proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of a Pharmacist-Led Diabetes Management Clinic with Remote Endocrinologist Consultation Versus Traditional In-Person Co-Management: A Pragmatic, Multi-Site Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated the adoption of telemedicine and highlighted the critical role of community pharmacists in public health delivery, including immunization. Chronic disease management, particularly for diabetes, was disrupted, creating a need for sustainable, accessible care models. This study leverages the established trust in community pharmacies to address specialist shortages, especially in rural areas, by testing a scalable team-based care model.
proposals.hypothesis: For patients with type 2 diabetes, a structured, pharmacist-led management clinic with asynchronous remote endocrinologist consultation will achieve non-inferior glycemic control (HbA1c reduction) and medication adherence compared to traditional in-person co-management by a pharmacist and endocrinologist.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, pragmatic cohort study comparing two care models over 12 months. The intervention arm will receive care at a pharmacist-led clinic in a community pharmacy setting, using a structured protocol for assessment and titration, with complex cases reviewed via a secure platform by an endocrinologist. The control arm will receive standard in-person co-management. The primary outcome is change in HbA1c. Secondary outcomes include medication adherence (measured via pharmacy refill data), hospitalizations, and patient satisfaction. We will use propensity score matching to adjust for confounders and electronic health record data extraction, applying techniques similar to electronic phenotyping used in antimicrobial stewardship research, to define cohorts and outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust, real-world evidence on the effectiveness of a decentralized, pharmacy-based chronic care model, directly informing health system strategies for diabetes management post-pandemic. It will demonstrate how to operationalize a collaborative practice agreement at scale, potentially increasing access to specialist-guided care in underserved communities.
proposals.novelty: While pharmacist involvement in diabetes care is established, this proposal uniquely tests a fully pharmacist-operated clinic with remote specialist backup as a primary care delivery system, rather than an adjunct. It formally applies a 'task-shifting' framework, common in global health and tested in antimicrobial stewardship for decision support, to a high-prevalence chronic disease in a U.S. community pharmacy setting.

proposals.title: Comparing Intensive vs. Extended Support Models for Post-Discharge Antibiotic Adherence in Community-Acquired Pneumonia: A Stewardship Intervention Trial
proposals.motivation: During the COVID-19 pandemic, community pharmacies became critical hubs for patient support and immunization, highlighting their role in public health interventions. For antimicrobial stewardship, ensuring patient adherence to prescribed oral antibiotics after discharge for conditions like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) is vital to prevent treatment failure and resistance, but the optimal support model is unknown. This addresses a gap in outpatient stewardship where long-term outcomes are often neglected.
proposals.hypothesis: A less intense, longer-duration pharmacist-led support model will yield equivalent or superior long-term antibiotic adherence and clinical outcomes compared to a standard, intense short-term intervention for patients discharged with CAP.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, randomized controlled trial at community pharmacies. Adults discharged with a diagnosis of CAP and a prescription for oral antibiotics will be randomized to: 1) Intensive Support (standard of care: detailed in-pharmacy counseling at pickup and a 48-hour follow-up call) or 2) Extended Support (brief initial counseling plus scheduled weekly check-in calls/texts for the full antibiotic course). Adherence will be measured primarily via pharmacy refill data and electronic pill monitors (MEMS caps). Secondary outcomes include 30-day readmission and symptom resolution. Analysis will use intention-to-treat and generalized linear mixed models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence-based guidance on the format of pharmacy-based stewardship interventions in the post-pandemic era, directly informing outpatient ASP strategies. It will generate novel data on the longitudinal adherence patterns for CAP therapy beyond the hospital setting, linking intervention intensity to real-world outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While electronic phenotyping identifies CAP inpatients for stewardship, and pharmacy-based interventions were studied for COVID-19 vaccines, the application of different longitudinal support models from community pharmacies to improve antibiotic adherence is a novel, patient-centered extension of stewardship principles into the post-discharge continuum.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Direct Medication Cost Savings of Syndrome-Specific Antimicrobial Stewardship for Community-Acquired Pneumonia During the COVID-19 Pandemic
proposals.motivation: During the COVID-19 pandemic, antimicrobial stewardship programs (ASPs) focused heavily on preventing complications like C. difficile infection and resistance. However, the direct cost savings from optimizing initial antibiotic selection—such as avoiding overly broad empiric therapy—are poorly quantified. This is critical for justifying ASP resources in community settings, including pharmacies, which played an expanded role in pandemic care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of total cost savings from an inpatient pneumonia ASP is derived directly from reduced medication acquisition costs via optimized, syndrome-specific empiric therapy, rather than solely from the avoidance of downstream complications.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental, pre-post study using electronic health record data from a multi-hospital system. Building on prior electronic phenotyping work for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), we will apply a validated algorithm to identify hospitalized CAP patients from 2019-2021. For the intervention period, we will define a cohort receiving ASP-guided, syndrome-specific empiric therapy (e.g., ceftriaxone + azithromycin). A matched historical control cohort will receive broader-spectrum therapy (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime). Primary outcomes are direct antibiotic acquisition cost per episode and total inpatient antibiotic days of therapy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a granular, medication-specific cost analysis for a syndrome-focused ASP, directly informing hospital and pharmacy budget decisions. It will also demonstrate the economic value of integrating electronic phenotyping into stewardship, particularly relevant for pandemic and post-pandemic resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links ASPs to reduced complications, this study specifically isolates and quantifies the direct drug cost savings of syndrome-specific empiric optimization, a component often overshadowed in economic analyses. It uniquely applies this framework to the COVID-19 era, where antibiotic use patterns were disrupted.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in Cost-Effectiveness of a Pharmacy-Based Community-Acquired Pneumonia Management Program: A Subgroup Analysis Using Electronic Phenotyping and Microbiological Data
proposals.motivation: During and after the COVID-19 pandemic, community pharmacies expanded their role in managing acute infections like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) to improve access and stewardship. While such programs are often evaluated for average effectiveness, their cost-effectiveness may not be uniform across all patient subgroups, leading to inefficient resource allocation. Identifying subgroups for whom the program is not cost-effective is crucial for optimizing public health strategy and ensuring equitable care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a community pharmacy-based CAP management program demonstrates significant heterogeneity in cost-effectiveness, with specific patient subgroups—defined by microbiological etiology, immunocompromised status, and rurality—deriving less economic benefit, potentially making the program cost-ineffective for them.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked electronic health record, pharmacy claims, and microbiological data from a rural health network. The primary intervention is enrollment in a pharmacy-based CAP program. We will apply an established electronic phenotyping algorithm (similar to Ha's prior work) to identify CAP cases. Cost-effectiveness will be assessed via a decision-analytic model comparing program costs to outcomes (e.g., treatment failure, hospitalization). Patient subgroups will be defined a priori using classification and regression tree (CART) analysis, with key splits based on immunocompromised status (from Ha's bacteremia research), pathogen resistance profile (e.g., cefepime-non-susceptible isolates), and rurality/pharmacy access metrics (from Ha's pharmacy survey work).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for evaluating heterogeneity in public health interventions, moving beyond average effects. It will offer actionable insights for tailoring CAP management programs by identifying patient types who may require alternative care pathways. The findings will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship and rural health policy by highlighting where pharmacy-based programs are and are not economically justified.
proposals.novelty: While electronic phenotyping for stewardship exists, its integration with cost-effectiveness analysis and CART-based subgroup discovery for a community pharmacy intervention is novel. This approach directly challenges the one-size-fits-all assumption by leveraging the researcher's unique blend of microbiological and community pharmacy expertise from the pandemic era.

proposals.title: Predictors of Long-Term Adherence to Antimicrobial Regimens Following Hospital Discharge: A Machine Learning Analysis of Community and Pharmacy Factors in Post-COVID-19 Pneumonia Patients
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted healthcare delivery and patient engagement, particularly for conditions like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) requiring complex post-discharge antibiotic regimens. Poor adherence after intensive inpatient 'tune-up' leads to treatment failure, readmission, and antimicrobial resistance, yet key predictors in the post-pandemic community setting are poorly characterized. This gap is critical for stewardship programs aiming to extend their impact beyond the hospital.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of patient-level socioeconomic factors, post-discharge pharmacy accessibility, and specific antimicrobial agent (e.g., cefepime vs. broader-spectrum alternatives) will be significant, non-linear predictors of long-term adherence, identifiable via electronic phenotyping and machine learning.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from patients hospitalized with CAP (defined by an established electronic phenotype) who received a 'tune-up' intervention. Adherence will be measured via pharmacy refill data. Predictors will include demographics, comorbidities, discharge antibiotic class, distance to pharmacy, and community pharmacy immunization service utilization (a proxy for engagement, per pre-2026 survey data). We will employ gradient boosting machines (XGBoost) to model adherence as a binary outcome, using SHAP values for interpretability to identify key non-linear predictive features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a data-driven model to identify patients at high risk for non-adherence, enabling targeted stewardship follow-up. It will extend the concept of electronic phenotyping from inpatient syndrome identification to post-discharge outcome prediction. The findings will inform the design of community pharmacy-based adherence interventions, bridging hospital care and public health.
proposals.novelty: While electronic phenotyping for CAP exists, its application to model post-discharge adherence outcomes is novel. Integrating pharmacy service utilization data—a community resource highlighted during the pandemic—with detailed microbiological and treatment data to predict adherence represents a new, pragmatic synthesis of the researcher's expertise.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness of Electronic Phenotyping for Antimicrobial Stewardship in Community-Acquired Pneumonia: A Comparative Analysis Across Medicaid, Medicare, and Independent Practice Settings
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare resources and highlighted disparities in care access and delivery. Economic evaluations of antimicrobial stewardship tools, like electronic phenotyping for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), are critical but often lack generalizability across diverse payer and practice settings, such as Medicaid populations or small independent practices with limited infrastructure. Understanding the cost-benefit profile in these varied contexts is essential for equitable implementation and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: The cost-benefit ratio of implementing electronic CAP phenotyping for inpatient antimicrobial stewardship will vary significantly across different payer systems (Medicaid vs. Medicare) and practice settings (small independent vs. large hospital-affiliated practices), with the greatest net benefit observed in high-volume, resource-constrained settings serving vulnerable populations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cost-benefit analysis using a pre-post intervention design. The intervention is the implementation of the previously validated electronic CAP phenotyping algorithm. Data on antibiotic use (e.g., days of therapy), clinical outcomes (length of stay, treatment failure), and costs will be extracted from electronic health records at sites representing distinct settings: a Medicaid-dominated safety-net hospital, a Medicare-managed academic center, and a consortium of small independent practices. A decision-analytic model will be built to compare scenario costs, incorporating direct medical costs and accounting for differences in reimbursement structures and operational overhead.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative economic analysis of a syndrome-specific antimicrobial stewardship tool across fundamentally different U.S. healthcare delivery models. It will generate actionable data for administrators and policymakers on where investments in electronic phenotyping are most economically justified, potentially guiding resource allocation to improve stewardship in underserved settings.
proposals.novelty: While electronic phenotyping for CAP has been validated clinically, its economic evaluation has not been contextualized within the specific financial and operational realities of Medicaid systems or small independent practices, which face unique cost pressures and implementation barriers compared to large integrated systems.

proposals.title: Microsimulation Modeling of Community Pharmacy-Based Antimicrobial Stewardship: Long-Term Projections for Post-Pandemic Pneumonia Outcomes and Resistance
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated the role of community pharmacies in public health, including immunization and potentially antimicrobial stewardship. However, the long-term population-level impact of integrating syndrome-specific stewardship (e.g., for community-acquired pneumonia) into these decentralized settings is unknown. Forecasting these outcomes is critical to justify investment and guide policy to combat antimicrobial resistance and improve care continuity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a microsimulation model will project that widespread adoption of a pharmacy-based antimicrobial stewardship intervention for community-acquired pneumonia will significantly reduce inappropriate antibiotic exposure and downstream incidence of resistant gram-negative bacteremia at a population level over a 10-year horizon.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete-time, state-transition microsimulation model in R using the `microsimulation` package or similar, informed by pre-2026 data. The model will simulate a synthetic U.S. population with individual attributes (age, comorbidities, healthcare utilization). Key transitions will include pneumonia episodes, encounters with clinics/pharmacies, antibiotic prescriptions (modeling choices like cefepime vs. broader agents), and development of resistant infections (e.g., cefoxitin-non-susceptible Enterobacterales). Calibration targets will be derived from the researcher's prior work on electronic phenotyping and bacteremia outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, long-term forecast of a decentralized, pharmacy-integrated antimicrobial stewardship strategy. It will offer policymakers and health systems a tool to evaluate the potential return on investment of expanding stewardship beyond hospitals. The model will also serve as a framework for assessing other outpatient syndrome-specific interventions.
proposals.novelty: While microsimulation is established in health economics, its application to project outcomes of community pharmacy-based antimicrobial stewardship, synthesizing real-world data on pneumonia phenotyping and resistant bacteremia, is novel. This bridges the researcher's expertise in clinical microbiology, community pharmacy practice during COVID-19, and inpatient stewardship.

proposals.title: Quantifying Patient Preferences for Post-Pandemic Respiratory Infection Care: A Discrete Choice Experiment on Clinic Attributes and Telehealth Features
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic permanently altered healthcare delivery, accelerating telehealth adoption and shifting patient expectations for managing community-acquired infections like pneumonia. Understanding patient priorities is critical for designing sustainable, patient-centered care models that improve access and adherence, especially in rural and underserved communities where pharmacy-based services expanded during the pandemic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for the management of suspected community-acquired respiratory infections, patient choices are influenced more by specific telehealth features (e.g., rapid test result communication) and convenience attributes (e.g., pharmacy-based testing) than by traditional factors like clinic reputation, and that these preferences vary significantly between urban and rural populations.
proposals.method: We will employ a discrete choice experiment (DCE), a conjoint analysis method from marketing. Attributes and levels (e.g., consultation mode: in-person, video, phone; result wait time: 1hr, 24hrs, 3 days; test location: clinic, drive-through, pharmacy; cost) will be identified via literature review and patient focus groups. A D-efficient experimental design will generate choice sets. We will recruit a stratified sample (urban/rural) of patients with a history of respiratory infection care. Statistical analysis will use mixed logit models to estimate preference weights and willingness-to-trade metrics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, trade-off-based evidence of patient preferences for the clinical pathway of community-acquired respiratory infection management in the post-pandemic era. It will directly inform the design of outpatient and antimicrobial stewardship programs by identifying which service delivery features maximize patient uptake and satisfaction, bridging a gap between clinical efficacy and patient-centered implementation.
proposals.novelty: While DCEs are established in health economics, their application to operationalize 'electronic phenotyping' for stewardship by designing patient-acceptable care pathways is novel. This integrates marketing science with infectious diseases outcomes research to optimize real-world service design post-COVID-19.

proposals.title: A Blueprint for National Implementation: Scaling a Community Pharmacy-Based Immunization and Antimicrobial Stewardship Model Through a Major U.S. Payer Network
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic demonstrated the critical role of community pharmacies in expanding access to immunizations and basic care, particularly in underserved areas. However, sustained, scalable models that integrate these services with antimicrobial stewardship to reduce inappropriate antibiotic use for syndromes like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) remain underdeveloped. A national payer-led program could provide the financial and structural framework to institutionalize this integrated public health approach beyond the pandemic emergency phase.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured implementation blueprint, co-designed with a major national insurer and its network pharmacies, will be feasible and can be modeled to significantly increase adult immunization rates and reduce inappropriate antibiotic prescriptions for CAP-like syndromes across diverse geographic regions.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-phase, mixed-methods design. First, we will conduct key informant interviews and surveys with pharmacy leaders and insurer representatives to identify barriers and facilitators to scaling. Second, we will adapt electronic phenotyping algorithms for CAP (based on Ha's prior work) into a simplified screening tool for community pharmacy use. Third, using insurer claims data and geospatial mapping, we will create a simulation model to project the impact of different rollout strategies (e.g., phased by pharmacy density, rurality, or baseline immunization rates) on program reach, cost, and clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, data-driven implementation guide for payers to deploy integrated vaccination and syndrome-specific stewardship programs nationally. It will advance the field of outpatient antimicrobial stewardship by demonstrating a scalable, financially sustainable model that leverages existing community infrastructure and payer data systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has documented pharmacy-based services in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates immunization with electronic phenotyping for stewardship and designs the rollout from the pivotal perspective of a national payer, whose adoption is essential for large-scale, permanent implementation.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of an Electronic Phenotype for High-Risk Post-COVID Patients to Prioritize Referral to a Multidisciplinary Tune-Up Clinic
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic has created a large population of patients with post-acute sequelae (PASC) and complex, fragmented care needs, overwhelming specialist capacity. Community-based interventions like tune-up clinics are resource-intensive, necessitating a systematic method to identify patients who would derive the greatest clinical benefit from such services, thereby optimizing impact and healthcare efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: An electronic phenotype derived from routine clinical and pharmacy data can accurately identify patients with a high burden of unresolved post-COVID complications and healthcare utilization who are most likely to benefit from a multidisciplinary tune-up clinic intervention.
proposals.method: We will apply a supervised machine learning approach, using a retrospective cohort of post-COVID patients. The outcome will be a composite of post-referral outcomes (e.g., reduced hospitalization, improved symptom scores). Predictors will include features from electronic health records (diagnoses, medications, lab results, prior hospitalization for pneumonia/bacteremia) and community pharmacy data (immunization status, antibiotic fills). We will train and compare models, including logistic regression, random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), using pre-2026 libraries (e.g., scikit-learn), to create a risk-stratification tool.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, scalable screening tool for healthcare systems to efficiently allocate tune-up clinic resources. It extends antimicrobial stewardship-style electronic phenotyping to the domain of post-pandemic care coordination, creating a methodology for targeting complex chronic disease management.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the typical clinic referral process by proactively identifying high-need patients using data, rather than relying on provider recognition. It uniquely integrates community pharmacy data with inpatient microbiological/antibiotic history—domains from the researcher's prior work—to create a holistic risk profile for post-COVID care escalation.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Multi-Agent Ensemble Learning Framework for Patient-Specific Risk Stratification of Bacteremia Complications in Community-Acquired Infections
proposals.motivation: Bacteremia, a leading cause of sepsis and mortality, presents heterogeneously, complicating risk assessment and antibiotic stewardship. During the COVID-19 pandemic, community healthcare settings, including pharmacies, became critical but resource-limited points of care, heightening the need for precise, adaptable risk tools. Existing monolithic predictive models lack the flexibility to account for diverse patient subpopulations (e.g., immunocompromised) and data patterns, limiting their clinical utility in dynamic, real-world settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic ensemble of specialized machine learning agents, selectively activated based on individual patient profiles, will provide more accurate and clinically interpretable predictions of bacteremia complications (e.g., septic shock, treatment failure) than a single, static model.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where distinct ML 'agents' (e.g., gradient boosting machines for tabular data, temporal convolutional networks for vital sign trends, and attention-based models for comorbidity pathways) are pre-trained on curated subsets of a multi-center electronic health record cohort of community-onset bacteremia. For each new patient, a gating network, using pre-2026 meta-learning techniques, will dynamically select and weight a relevant subset of agents based on presenting features. Predictions from this tailored committee will be aggregated via learned or adaptive weighting schemes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, adaptive risk modeling tool that can support inpatient syndrome-specific antimicrobial stewardship programs by providing nuanced, patient-level risk assessments. It will advance methodological research in clinical ML by demonstrating how model complexity can be dynamically matched to case complexity, potentially improving both predictive performance and model interpretability for clinicians.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond static ensemble methods by dynamically constructing a unique model committee for each patient, mimicking clinical reasoning through the selective integration of specialized diagnostic perspectives. This adaptive complexity is a significant departure from the one-model-fits-all paradigm dominant in pre-2026 clinical prediction literature.

proposals.title: A Multi-Agent Simulation of Stakeholder Behavior to Optimize IV-to-Oral Transition for Community-Acquired Pneumonia: A Tool for Post-Pandemic Antimicrobial Stewardship
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare resources and disrupted standard care pathways, including antimicrobial stewardship. Transitioning from IV to oral antibiotics for conditions like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) is a proven, cost-effective stewardship strategy, but real-world adoption is hindered by complex stakeholder behaviors (e.g., physician habit, patient preference, pharmacy access, institutional policy). Static economic models fail to capture these dynamic interactions, limiting the implementation of personalized, cost-saving pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-agent simulation that explicitly models the incentives and decision-making of patients, physicians, pharmacists, and hospital administrators will dynamically identify personalized IV-to-oral transition pathways that are both cost-effective and have high predicted adherence in real-world clinical settings.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model using a framework like Mesa (Python). Agents will be programmed with behavioral rules derived from pre-2026 literature on stewardship, health economics, and our team's work on electronic phenotyping of CAP. Their decision-making will incorporate techniques from game theory and opponent modeling (e.g., level-k reasoning) to predict responses to policy changes. The model will be calibrated and validated using retrospective data on CAP management, including pharmacy records and clinical outcomes, to simulate the impact of different intervention strategies on cost and protocol adherence.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, dynamic policy simulation tool that moves beyond static cost-effectiveness analysis. It will provide actionable insights for designing stewardship interventions that account for human behavior, directly addressing a key implementation gap. The framework will be a generalizable model for optimizing other clinical transitions where stakeholder alignment is critical.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying opponent/teammate modeling from multi-agent systems to simulate and influence the real-world behavioral economics of antimicrobial stewardship, creating an adaptive policy tool rather than a static recommendation.

proposals.title: A Game-Theoretic Model for Diagnostic De-escalation: Determining Nash Equilibria for Forgoing Repeat Blood Cultures in Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: During the COVID-19 pandemic, optimizing resource use in community and hospital settings became critical. In bloodstream infections, obtaining follow-up blood cultures to confirm clearance is standard but resource-intensive; however, it is unclear when this test is truly necessary versus a reflexive practice. A principled framework to personalize this decision could enhance antimicrobial stewardship and reduce healthcare burdens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the clinical decision to forgo a repeat blood culture can be modeled as a Nash equilibrium in a sequential game between clinician and pathogen, and that this equilibrium is predictable based on specific microbiological, pharmacological, and host factors.
proposals.method: We will construct a two-player, sequential game model where the clinician chooses to culture or not, and the pathogen state (cleared/persistent) is a probabilistic outcome influenced by treatment and host immunity. Using retrospective data from bacteremia patients (including pathogen ID, antibiotic susceptibility, cefepime/piperacillin-tazobactam use, and immunocompromised status), we will employ maximum likelihood estimation to parameterize payoff functions (e.g., cost of treatment failure vs. test). Backward induction will be used to solve for Nash equilibria across different clinical scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mathematically rigorous framework to guide personalized diagnostic de-escalation in bacteremia management. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship programs by identifying patient-specific scenarios where repeat cultures are rationally unnecessary, potentially reducing lab costs and antibiotic overuse.
proposals.novelty: While game theory has been applied to antibiotic resistance policy, its use to model the tactical, diagnostic duel between a single clinician and a pathogen population in real-time patient management is novel. This moves beyond population-level guidelines to a dynamic, patient-stratified decision model.

proposals.title: Decentralized Antimicrobial Decision-Making in a Multi-Agent Simulation of Community-Acquired Infection During an Epidemic
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained diagnostic capacity, forcing empiric treatment decisions based on syndromic surveillance, as seen in electronic phenotyping for pneumonia. In community-acquired bacteremia, timely empiric therapy is critical, but pathogen identification is delayed. This creates a partially observable environment where decentralized actors (e.g., community pharmacies, clinics) must act on local, noisy data without confirmed source attribution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a population of simple, interacting agents, using only local syndromic data and minimalist rules, can self-organize to produce emergent, system-wide treatment protocols whose aggregate efficacy rivals that of a centralized system requiring confirmed microbiological diagnosis.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent simulation using a framework like Mesa (pre-2026). Agents represent community healthcare nodes (pharmacies, clinics) with rules derived from pre-2026 antimicrobial stewardship logic (e.g., syndromic classification akin to electronic phenotyping). The environment simulates an epidemic with multiple bacterial pathogens (e.g., Enterobacterales with varying resistance profiles). Agents receive noisy, local case data and can share limited signals. We will test if coordination emerges using metrics like overall treatment failure rates, comparing emergent protocols to benchmark centralized strategies requiring confirmed diagnosis (e.g., piperacillin/tazobactam vs. cefepime logic).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for resilient, decentralized public health decision-making during diagnostic uncertainty. It will contribute a novel simulation framework for testing antimicrobial stewardship strategies under information constraints, directly informing the design of community-based response systems for future epidemics.
proposals.novelty: While multi-agent systems exist, applying them to model the emergent coordination of *syndromic antimicrobial decisions* under partial observability is novel. It fuses concepts from decentralized AI with practical, pre-2026 challenges in community-acquired infection management during a pandemic.

proposals.title: Patient Demand as a Primary Driver of Inappropriate Antibiotic Prescribing: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Community Pharmacy Encounters During and After the COVID-19 Pandemic
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship programs (ASPs) primarily target prescriber behavior, yet inappropriate antibiotic use remains high. The COVID-19 pandemic profoundly altered healthcare-seeking behaviors and patient expectations, particularly in community settings like pharmacies. Understanding patient-level drivers is critical for designing effective, patient-centered stewardship interventions that address the root causes of demand.
proposals.hypothesis: Patient demand and cultural expectations for antibiotics, amplified by pandemic-era healthcare disruptions, are a stronger primary driver of inappropriate antibiotic prescribing in community settings than prescriber preference alone, and this demand can be quantitatively characterized through patient-pharmacist interactions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a sequential mixed-methods study. First, we will perform electronic phenotyping of antibiotic requests at community pharmacies using a natural language processing (NLP) algorithm (based on BERT architecture) applied to anonymized pharmacy consultation notes and linked prescription data from a regional health information exchange to quantify demand patterns. Second, we will administer a structured survey, adapted from the researcher's prior work, to community pharmacists in a multi-state region to qualitatively assess their perceptions of patient demand, pressure, and cultural factors influencing antibiotic dispensing during and after the pandemic's acute phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide novel empirical evidence quantifying the role of patient demand in antibiotic misuse, moving stewardship focus beyond the prescriber. It will generate a scalable, electronic phenotyping tool for monitoring community-level antibiotic demand signals. The findings will directly inform the design of patient-facing stewardship interventions deployable in community pharmacies.
proposals.novelty: While electronic phenotyping has been applied to inpatient syndromes, its use to model patient demand in community pharmacies is novel. Integrating quantitative NLP analysis of real-world data with qualitative survey data from pharmacists directly bridges the researcher's expertise in microbiological outcomes, pharmacy-based services, and pandemic-era community health.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Decision Framework for Antimicrobial Stewardship in Community-Acquired Pneumonia with Diagnostic Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship (AS) programs rely on binary definitions of 'appropriate' use, which fail in common clinical scenarios with diagnostic uncertainty, such as community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) where viral vs. bacterial etiology is unclear. This ambiguity, heightened during the COVID-19 pandemic with overlapping respiratory syndromes, forces stewardship interventions to be probabilistic. Current electronic phenotyping tools for CAP lack a formal framework to guide AS actions when appropriateness is not definitively knowable.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian decision framework that integrates pre-test probability, diagnostic test characteristics, and patient-specific risk/benefit ratios can optimize stewardship recommendations for CAP, reducing unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic use without increasing adverse outcomes compared to standard binary appropriateness rules.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian network model using electronic health record data from patients with CAP electronic phenotypes. The model will incorporate variables for clinical presentation, procalcitonin/C-reactive protein results, COVID-19 status, and local resistance patterns. We will compare stewardship actions (e.g., de-escalation, discontinuation) recommended by the Bayesian framework against a gold-standard adjudication panel's probabilistic assessment. The model's performance will be validated in a retrospective cohort of immunocompromised patients with bacteremia risk, a group where diagnostic uncertainty is high.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, transparent model for AS decision-making under uncertainty, moving beyond rigid 'appropriate/inappropriate' classifications. It will offer a tool for stewardship teams to justify probabilistic interventions and could be integrated into existing electronic phenotyping systems for CAP and other syndromes.
proposals.novelty: While electronic phenotyping identifies syndromes, it does not prescribe action under uncertainty. This proposal uniquely combines Bayesian decision theory with real-world AS challenges, explicitly relaxing the core assumption that appropriateness is always a binary, definable endpoint.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of Syndromic Stewardship Metrics for Community-Acquired Infections in Non-Acute Care Settings
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship programs (ASPs) are well-established in hospitals, but validated outcome metrics for clinics and nursing homes are lacking. This gap is critical as these settings are major sources of antibiotic use and patient interaction, especially highlighted during the COVID-19 pandemic with the expansion of pharmacy-based care. Without specific, actionable metrics, stewardship efforts in community settings cannot be rigorously evaluated or improved.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a set of syndromic, process-based metrics, adapted from inpatient electronic phenotyping methods and applied to non-acute care electronic health record (EHR) data, can be feasibly measured and will correlate with improved antibiotic prescribing patterns for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) and urinary tract infections (UTIs).
proposals.method: Leveraging the researcher's experience with electronic phenotyping for CAP, we will develop algorithms to identify CAP and UTI episodes in the EHR data of a network of community clinics and nursing homes. We will define and validate a core metric set, including syndrome-specific appropriateness (e.g., antibiotic selection per guidelines), duration, and follow-up culture utilization. Validation will involve manual chart review for a subset of cases and correlation analysis with aggregate facility-level prescribing quality indicators.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, syndromic stewardship metric toolkit specifically designed for the data streams and clinical workflows of non-acute care. It will provide a necessary tool for ASPs in these settings to measure their impact and guide interventions, directly extending stewardship principles from hospitals into the community.
proposals.novelty: While electronic phenotyping exists for inpatient stewardship, its adaptation to create validated outcome measures for the syndromic stewardships of CAP and UTI in clinics and nursing homes is novel. This bridges the researcher's expertise in microbiological outcomes, community pharmacy roles during COVID-19, and electronic phenotyping.

proposals.title: Impact of an EHR-Integrated, Multidisciplinary Antimicrobial Stewardship Workflow on Antibiotic Selection and Clinical Outcomes in Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare systems, disrupting traditional antimicrobial stewardship (ASP) activities and highlighting the need for resilient, integrated approaches. Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) is a high-volume target for stewardship, yet pre-2026 EHR tools often lack formal, real-time workflows that actively engage pharmacists, microbiologists, and infectious disease (ID) specialists in a unified platform, leading to delays and suboptimal antibiotic choices.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured, EHR-embedded multidisciplinary workflow for CAP will improve guideline-concordant antibiotic selection and reduce the use of broad-spectrum agents (e.g., cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam) compared to standard, non-integrated stewardship practices.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a prospective, quasi-experimental study at a community hospital. The intervention is an EHR-native workflow triggered by an electronic CAP phenotype. It automatically pages the ASP pharmacist, displays relevant microbiology data (e.g., local susceptibility, blood culture alerts), and provides a structured consult form for ID specialist input. We will use interrupted time-series analysis to compare antibiotic days of therapy, time to optimal therapy, and clinical failure rates in the pre- and post-implementation periods, adjusting for patient covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the operational and clinical effectiveness of a formally integrated, multidisciplinary EHR workflow for syndrome-specific stewardship. It will yield a replicable technical blueprint for embedding collaborative ASP into routine care, potentially improving antibiotic use and patient outcomes in common infections like CAP.
proposals.novelty: While electronic phenotyping exists, this proposal uniquely engineers a real-time, multi-actor collaborative platform within the EHR itself, directly linking the diagnostic expertise of microbiology, the pharmacologic insight of pharmacy, and the clinical judgment of ID into a single, actionable stewardship event.

proposals.title: Nudging Prescribers in the CPOE: A Randomized Trial of Behavioral Economic Interventions to Optimize Empiric Antibiotic Selection for Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship programs (ASPs) face persistent challenges in changing prescriber behavior, especially for common infections like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP). During the COVID-19 pandemic, the strain on healthcare systems and shifts in care delivery (e.g., increased use of community pharmacies) highlighted the need for scalable, low-touch stewardship tools. Computerized provider order entry (CPOE) systems are ubiquitous but underutilized as active behavioral intervention platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing specific behavioral economic nudges (default options, active choice prompts, and social norming) within the CAP order set of a CPOE system will increase the proportion of guideline-concordant empiric antibiotic regimens without increasing alert fatigue.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial across multiple hospitals. Using the institution's existing CPOE and electronic phenotyping tools (similar to those in the researcher's prior work), we will identify patients meeting CAP criteria. For intervention clusters, the CAP order set will be redesigned with three nudges: 1) setting a guideline-recommended antibiotic as the pre-selected default, 2) requiring an active choice between guideline-concordant and alternative regimens, and 3) displaying a message stating '90% of your colleagues prescribe guideline-concordant therapy for CAP.' Control clusters will use the standard order set. The primary outcome is the rate of guideline-concordant prescribing.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence on the efficacy of choice architecture in inpatient antimicrobial stewardship. It will translate principles from behavioral economics into a concrete, implementable CPOE intervention, offering a scalable model to complement traditional ASP strategies. The findings will directly inform health IT design to promote high-value care.
proposals.novelty: While ASPs commonly use restrictions and post-prescription review, proactively engineering the choice architecture of CPOE for common syndromes is a novel application. This approach moves beyond passive decision support alerts to actively shape the prescribing environment, integrating stewardship into the clinical workflow seamlessly.

proposals.title: Optimizing Broad-Spectrum Antibiotic Inventory in Community Hospitals: A Supply Chain Management Approach to Stewardship
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted vulnerabilities in hospital supply chains and altered antibiotic prescribing patterns, potentially exacerbating antimicrobial resistance. Broad-spectrum antibiotics like piperacillin/tazobactam and cefepime are often overused, partly due to their ready availability in hospital formularies and stockpiles. Reducing unnecessary exposure to these agents is a critical stewardship goal to preserve their efficacy against pathogens like Enterobacterales.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying just-in-time (JIT) inventory principles and time-series demand forecasting models to broad-spectrum antibiotic supply will reduce on-hand stockpiles, which will be associated with a measurable decrease in their empirical use without compromising time-to-therapy for confirmed infections.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental study in a network of community hospitals. Using historical electronic health record data on antibiotic administrations and confirmed bacteremia cases (e.g., from prior phenotyping work), we will develop and validate a demand forecasting model (e.g., ARIMA or Prophet) for key broad-spectrum agents. This model will inform a revised, JIT-aligned inventory protocol implemented in the pharmacy. The primary outcome will be days of therapy (DOT) per 1000 patient-days for target antibiotics, compared pre- and post-intervention using interrupted time series analysis. Secondary outcomes include time-to-first-dose for culture-confirmed infections and financial waste from expired drugs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, operational framework for antimicrobial stewardship that directly targets the supply-side driver of overuse. It will generate evidence on how inventory management can be a tangible, non-prescriber-facing stewardship intervention. The project will also produce a validated forecasting tool adaptable to other hospital settings and antibiotic classes.
proposals.novelty: While supply chain models are standard in retail, their application to antibiotic stewardship is nascent. This approach uniquely bridges operational logistics with clinical outcomes, moving beyond traditional education- or restriction-based stewardship by structurally limiting availability to curb reflexive empirical use.

proposals.title: A Trigger-Based Protocol for Selective Repeat Blood Culture in Gram-Negative Bacteremia: A Stewardship Initiative Modeled on Oncologic Active Surveillance
proposals.motivation: Routine repeat blood cultures are a cornerstone of managing bacteremia but are often performed reflexively, incurring significant cost, laboratory resource use, and potential for false-positive results without clear evidence of benefit for all patients. During the COVID-19 pandemic, stewardship efforts focused on outpatient and community settings, but analogous resource-conscious, evidence-based protocols for inpatient diagnostic stewardship are underdeveloped. This creates an opportunity to reduce low-value care while maintaining patient safety.
proposals.hypothesis: For patients with monomicrobial Gram-negative bacteremia from a known urinary or biliary source, a structured protocol using specific clinical and biomarker triggers can safely identify a subset for whom scheduled repeat blood cultures can be omitted without increasing the risk of undetected treatment failure or metastatic infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult inpatients with E. coli, Klebsiella spp., or Proteus spp. bacteremia from 2019-2024. Using electronic health record data, we will apply an electronic phenotyping algorithm to define the index episode and source. We will then develop and validate a candidate "watchful waiting" rule-set, with triggers for repeat culture including persistent fever, rising procalcitonin or CRP after 48-72 hours of effective therapy, or new clinical signs of metastatic infection. The primary outcome will be a composite of 30-day mortality or readmission for related infection, comparing rule-identified low-risk patients who did not get repeat cultures (natural history) against those who did.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, protocolized framework for selective repeat blood culture, moving stewardship from antibiotic choice to diagnostic optimization. It will generate a concrete clinical decision tool that can be integrated into electronic health records to reduce low-value testing. The findings will establish safety benchmarks and resource savings estimates to guide future prospective trials.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel by directly translating the conceptual framework of active surveillance from oncology to infectious diseases diagnostics, a cross-disciplinary analogy not previously applied. It shifts the stewardship paradigm from *when to stop* antibiotics to *when to repeat* a diagnostic test, focusing on a high-volume, costly practice.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Multi-Agent System for Personalized Repeat Blood Culture Decisions in Bacteremia: A Safety and Efficacy Study
proposals.motivation: Standardized repeat blood culture protocols, while common, may lead to unnecessary testing or missed persistent bacteremia, especially in complex patients like those studied in Dr. Ha's prior work on immunocompromised hosts. The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare resources, highlighting the need for efficient, evidence-based diagnostic stewardship. This project addresses the critical gap in personalizing this high-stakes decision by leveraging multi-agent AI, a method not yet applied to this specific clinical workflow.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic multi-agent decision system, which adapts its ensemble size and composition based on patient complexity, can safely recommend forgoing repeat blood cultures in a significant proportion of cases without increasing the risk of adverse outcomes compared to a fixed protocol.
proposals.method: We will develop a system where specialized agents (Microbiology, Pharmacokinetics/Pharmacodynamics, Host Response, Epidemiology) are instantiated based on initial data (e.g., pathogen, antibiotic, immunocompromise status). Agents will use pre-2026 algorithms: a Bayesian belief network for the Microbiology agent, pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic models for the Pharmacology agent, and gradient-boosted decision trees for the Host agent. Their recommendations will be synthesized via a weighted voting mechanism, with the confidence threshold for omitting repeat culture dynamically adjusted by the number of active agents and their agreement. The system will be trained and validated on retrospective electronic health record data, including patients with bacteremia from the pre-COVID and COVID periods.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, explainable clinical decision support tool for diagnostic stewardship in bacteremia management. It will provide a formal framework for quantifying decision robustness in repeat testing and generate evidence on the safety of personalized, protocol-deviating recommendations. The findings could directly inform antimicrobial stewardship programs in both routine and resource-constrained settings.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond static rules or single-model predictions by explicitly modeling the clinical decision-making *process* as a dynamic, collaborative multi-agent system, where architectural complexity scales with patient-specific uncertainty, a concept novel to diagnostic test optimization.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of the Outpatient Immunocompromised Infection Safety (OIIS) Score for Community-Acquired Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated the shift of care to outpatient settings, yet immunocompromised patients with infections like bacteremia are routinely excluded from such pathways due to perceived high risk, leading to unnecessary hospitalizations. This broad exclusion fails to account for heterogeneity within this population, where some patients may have a sufficiently low risk of complications for safe home management. A precision risk-stratification tool is needed to safely expand outpatient stewardship, reducing hospital-acquired complications and resource strain.
proposals.hypothesis: A multidimensional risk score, integrating host factors, pathogen microbiology, and initial treatment response, can accurately identify a subset of immunocompromised patients with community-onset bacteremia who are at very low risk (<2%) for clinical deterioration, enabling safe outpatient management.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of immunocompromised adults with community-onset bacteremia (e.g., Enterobacterales) from 2018-2024, using electronic health record data and electronic phenotyping methods. Candidate predictors will include immunocompromise type/severity, comorbidity burden, pathogen and antibiotic susceptibility (e.g., cefepime vs. carbapenem necessity), vital sign stability at 24 hours, and pharmacy-based treatment access. We will use logistic regression with LASSO regularization to develop the OIIS Score, followed by internal validation and calibration. A subsequent pragmatic pilot trial will test a protocolized monitoring bundle, delivered via pharmacy and telehealth, using the score for enrollment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, operational tool to guide outpatient management decisions for immunocompromised patients with infection, moving beyond a blanket inpatient rule. It will generate a protocol for technology-supported monitoring with explicit escalation thresholds, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and care pathway design for a vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its specific application of precision risk-stratification to de-implement a deeply entrenched clinical rule (automatic hospitalization for immunocompromised bacteremia) and in its integration of real-time microbiological data (e.g., cefoxitin non-susceptibility patterns) and community pharmacy resources into a dynamic safety protocol.

proposals.title: A Precision Monitoring Framework for Risk Stratification of Immunocompromised Patients with Community-Acquired Infections: A Pilot Study to Define a 'Managed Tolerance' Phenotype
proposals.motivation: During the COVID-19 pandemic, community-based care pathways, including pharmacy-based interventions, were crucial for managing patient surges. However, immunocompromised patients are often automatically triaged to intensive, resource-heavy management for infections like bacteremia or pneumonia, regardless of their individual clinical stability. This blanket approach can lead to unnecessary hospitalizations, broad-spectrum antibiotic overuse (e.g., carbapenems, piperacillin/tazobactam), and patient burden, highlighting a need for more personalized, data-driven risk stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: A sub-population of immunocompromised patients with well-controlled underlying conditions (e.g., HIV with undetectable viral load, stable drug-induced immunosuppression) can be identified through continuous multi-modal monitoring as having a 'managed tolerance' phenotype, and for this group, streamlined outpatient management of select community-acquired infections will be non-inferior to standard aggressive inpatient care.
proposals.method: We will adapt concepts from precision agriculture's continuous crop monitoring to patient care. A pilot cohort of immunocompromised patients in a community setting will be equipped with wearable devices (e.g., continuous pulse oximetry, heart rate) and use a digital platform for daily symptom and temperature logging. This will be integrated with scheduled point-of-care biomarker assays (e.g., CRP, procalcitonin). Using an electronic phenotyping algorithm based on pre-2026 work (e.g., rule-based classifiers from inpatient pneumonia phenotyping), we will define stability thresholds. Machine learning models (e.g., gradient boosting) will analyze this high-frequency data to identify patterns predictive of clinical decompensation versus sustained stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for precision, community-based management of immunocompromised hosts, directly extending antimicrobial stewardship principles into outpatient immunocompromised care. It aims to generate a validated monitoring protocol and digital phenotype ('managed tolerance') that could safely reduce hospital admissions and guide more tailored antibiotic therapy (e.g., enabling use of narrower-spectrum agents like cefepime in stable outpatients).
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard paradigm by proactively seeking low-risk immunocompromised states using continuous data, rather than assuming high risk. It novelly applies agricultural 'stress-tolerant crop' monitoring logic to human immunology and adapts electronic phenotyping methods, previously used for inpatients, to a dynamic community setting.

proposals.title: Modeling Dynamic Host-Drug Alliances in Community-Acquired Infection Management: A Game-Theoretic Approach to Adaptive Antimicrobial Stewardship
proposals.motivation: Current antimicrobial stewardship relies on static risk stratification (e.g., immunocompromised vs. immunocompetent), which fails to capture the dynamic interplay between a patient's evolving immune state, pathogen kinetics, and drug action. This is critical in managing infections like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) and bacteremia, where overly broad or misaligned therapy can drive resistance or treatment failure, a problem exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic's strain on outpatient and community pharmacy resources.
proposals.hypothesis: A patient's effective defensive capacity during an infection can be modeled as forming context-specific, temporary alliances with certain antimicrobials or supportive measures, and that identifying the rules of these alliances will enable more precise, adaptive treatment protocols than static classifications.
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-agent reinforcement learning (RL) framework, treating the host immune response, pathogen, and antimicrobial therapy as agents in a partially observable stochastic game. Using retrospective electronic health record data from patients with CAP and Enterobacterales bacteremia (leveraging prior electronic phenotyping methods), we will train the model to identify optimal state-dependent treatment policies. Alliance rules will be derived from the learned policy graphs, focusing on scenarios where bacteriostatic drugs or dose adjustments form cooperative equilibria with a modulated host response, potentially enabling safe outpatient management.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational model for infection management that moves beyond fixed algorithms to adaptive protocols. It will generate testable clinical hypotheses about 'host-drug alliances,' potentially identifying new outpatient treatment pathways for infections that currently require hospitalization, thereby advancing antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the paradigm of static immune classification by framing infection management as a dynamic, multi-agent game, explicitly modeling cooperative and competitive interactions between host, drug, and pathogen—a perspective not captured in existing comparative effectiveness or stewardship research.

proposals.title: Risk-Stratified Management of Gram-Negative Bacteremia in Select Immunocompromised Patients: A Retrospective Cohort Study to Identify Candidates for Outpatient or Early Discharge Pathways
proposals.motivation: Current guidelines for managing Gram-negative bacteremia (GNB) often mandate prolonged hospitalization and broad-spectrum antibiotics for all immunocompromised patients, regardless of infection severity. This conservative approach, prevalent during the COVID-19 pandemic to protect vulnerable patients, strains healthcare resources and increases risks of hospital-acquired infections and antimicrobial resistance. A more nuanced, risk-stratified approach is needed to safely de-escalate care for a subset of these patients.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant subset of immunocompromised patients with community-onset, uncomplicated Gram-negative bacteremia, particularly from urinary or biliary sources, can be managed safely with early transition to oral therapy and close outpatient monitoring, without increased risk of treatment failure or mortality compared to immunocompetent patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2024. The cohort will include adults with E. coli, Klebsiella spp., or Proteus mirabilis bacteremia. We will apply an electronic phenotyping algorithm, similar to those used for pneumonia stewardship, to precisely define 'uncomplicated' bacteremia (e.g., source controlled, hemodynamically stable, no metastatic foci). Using multivariable logistic regression, we will model the association between specific immunocompromised states (e.g., solid tumor on chemotherapy, rheumatoid arthritis on biologics) and a composite outcome of 30-day treatment failure (death, recurrence, re-admission), adjusting for source, pathogen, and antimicrobial therapy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative evidence to support a paradigm shift from blanket inpatient management to risk-stratified care for immunocompromised hosts with bacteremia. It will generate a clinically actionable risk prediction tool to identify patients eligible for outpatient parenteral antibiotic therapy (OPAT) or early oral switch, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and bed management protocols.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard host immune status criterion by actively seeking to identify immunocompromised patients for less intensive management, rather than universally escalating their care. It moves beyond comparing antibiotic regimens to fundamentally re-evaluating the care setting and duration.

proposals.title: A Response-Guided, Risk-Stratified Protocol for Managing Presumed Source-Controlled Infections in the Community: A Pragmatic Trial Leveraging Community Pharmacy Networks
proposals.motivation: During the COVID-19 pandemic, management of infections shifted towards the community, increasing reliance on clinical judgment for presumed source control (e.g., drained abscess, treated UTI). However, misclassifying an uncontrolled source as 'uncomplicated' leads to delayed intervention, treatment failure, and bacteremia. Existing antimicrobial stewardship tools, like electronic phenotyping for pneumonia, lack dynamic, response-based pathways for these ambiguous cases, creating a critical patient safety gap.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a standardized, time-limited monitoring protocol with explicit failure criteria for patients with presumed source control will safely reduce unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic use and invasive procedures, while systematically identifying occult sources requiring escalation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a stepped-wedge, pragmatic trial in community pharmacy networks across five southeastern US states, building on established pharmacy-based immunization infrastructure. Adults initiated on oral antibiotics for a presumed source-controlled infection will be enrolled. A risk-stratified algorithm, incorporating pre-2026 biomarkers (e.g., procalcitonin trend, CRP) and clinical criteria (fever, pain, WBC), will guide scheduled pharmacist assessments at 24, 48, and 72 hours. Failure to meet predefined improvement criteria triggers an electronic alert to the prescriber for immediate advanced imaging/surgical consult. The primary outcome is a composite of clinical failure or escalation by 7 days, compared to usual care.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based, operational protocol for managing the 'grey zone' of presumed source control, directly extending inpatient stewardship principles to the community setting. It will demonstrate how integrated community pharmacy networks can function as a real-time monitoring system for infection outcomes, a model accelerated by the pandemic.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond static electronic phenotyping by introducing a dynamic, response-guided decision algorithm. It uniquely leverages the community pharmacy—a frequent post-discharge and pandemic care touchpoint—as the active node for protocol execution and safety monitoring, a novel application of this clinical workforce.

proposals.title: A Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process for Adaptive Antibiotic Stewardship in Bacteremia Under Source Control Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: During the COVID-19 pandemic, healthcare delivery was disrupted, increasing reliance on community pharmacies and complicating definitive source control for infections like bacteremia. Current antimicrobial stewardship often hinges on a binary, confirmed source control status, which can delay optimal treatment or lead to unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic use. A principled framework is needed to guide multi-specialty teams in managing infection when source control is uncertain but probable.
proposals.hypothesis: A Dec-POMDP model, trained on retrospective clinical data, can learn a joint policy for a treatment team that dynamically optimizes antibiotic strategy (e.g., de-escalation from cefepime) and diagnostic aggressiveness based on a collective belief state of source control likelihood, improving outcomes compared to static protocols.
proposals.method: We will formalize the surgeon, intensivist, and ID specialist as decentralized agents with local observations (e.g., imaging reports, clinical exam, microbiological data). Using a multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm, such as QMIX or MADDPG, we will train the team's joint policy on electronic health record data from patients with bacteremia. The action space includes antibiotic choices and diagnostic orders, and the reward function will balance clinical cure, antimicrobial spectrum, and cost. The model will be validated using a separate retrospective cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for syndrome-specific antimicrobial stewardship that explicitly models team decision-making under uncertainty. It will generate a testable, adaptive protocol for managing bacteremia when source control is not definitively confirmed, potentially reducing unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic use and optimizing resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: This fuses the clinical concept of relaxing definitive source control requirements with the Dec-POMDP formalism, moving beyond static phenotyping to a dynamic, team-based AI model that learns communication and action policies to manage diagnostic and therapeutic risk jointly.

proposals.title: Outcomes of Patients with Presumed Source Control for Complicated Infections: A Retrospective Cohort Study to Guide Antimicrobial Duration
proposals.motivation: During the COVID-19 pandemic, definitive source control (e.g., drainage, device removal) for infections like bacteremia or complicated pneumonia was sometimes delayed or presumed based on clinical improvement rather than confirmed imaging. This creates clinical uncertainty, often leading to prolonged antibiotic courses. A data-driven approach is needed to determine if infections with presumed source control can be safely managed with shorter, uncomplicated infection treatment regimens.
proposals.hypothesis: For immunocompromised patients with bacteremia from organisms like Enterobacter cloacae or Serratia marcescens, clinical outcomes following a presumed source control strategy (based on clinical and laboratory markers) are non-inferior to outcomes following confirmed radiographic or surgical source control when treated with standard antibiotic durations for uncomplicated infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data. An electronic phenotyping algorithm, building on Dr. Ha's prior work on pneumonia, will identify hospitalized patients (2019-2024) with bacteremia from target organisms and a documented infectious source (e.g., intra-abdominal, urinary). The algorithm will classify episodes as having 'confirmed' (via procedural/imaging documentation) or 'presumed' (clinical improvement without definitive intervention) source control. Primary outcome is a composite of 30-day infection recurrence, readmission, or mortality. We will use propensity score matching and multivariable regression to compare outcomes between groups treated with shorter-course (7-10 day) antibiotic therapy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence to support more precise, syndrome-specific antimicrobial stewardship. It could establish a framework for using electronic phenotyping to define a 'presumed source control' patient cohort, enabling safer de-escalation of therapy. The findings would directly inform clinical guidelines on antibiotic duration for complicated infections in resource-constrained or pandemic settings.
proposals.novelty: While source control is a cornerstone of infection management, the operational definition and required level of confirmation are poorly standardized. This study explicitly tests the sufficiency of a clinical definition of source control, a pragmatic question highlighted by pandemic care disruptions but not systematically studied in bacteremia.

proposals.title: Optimizing Antimicrobial Duration and Agent Count: A Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Model for Uncomplicated Community-Acquired Infections
proposals.motivation: Standardized, fixed-duration monotherapy for uncomplicated infections, while pragmatic, may contribute to unnecessary antimicrobial exposure, resistance selection, and patient burden. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need for adaptable, community-based treatment strategies and the value of pharmacy-led care. This project challenges the universality of the seven-day, single-agent paradigm by investigating how dynamically adjusting the number and sequence of agents can achieve non-inferior outcomes with shorter, personalized courses.
proposals.hypothesis: For common uncomplicated infections (e.g., community-acquired pneumonia, urinary tract infections), adaptive treatment schedules that dynamically adjust the number of antimicrobial agents (monotherapy, sequential therapy, or short-course combination) can yield non-inferior clinical outcomes compared to standard fixed-duration monotherapy, while reducing total antimicrobial exposure.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) model where each antimicrobial agent is an independent actor. The model will be trained on retrospective electronic health record data, incorporating microbiological susceptibility profiles (e.g., cefepime, piperacillin/tazobactam), host factors (immunocompromised status), and pharmacy dispensing data. Using a Deep Q-Network (DQN) architecture within a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) framework, the model will learn optimal policies for initiating, sequencing, and discontinuing agents to maximize a reward function balancing clinical cure and minimized agent-days.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational framework for personalized, syndrome-specific antimicrobial stewardship, directly extending electronic phenotyping methods. It will generate testable, minimal-duration regimen hypotheses for clinical trials and provide a proof-of-concept for adaptive multi-agent scheduling that could be deployed in community pharmacy settings to optimize outpatient therapy.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond comparing fixed regimens to modeling therapy as a dynamic, multi-agent system where duration is an emergent property of agent interactions and patient response, a significant departure from current comparative effectiveness research in antimicrobial therapy.

proposals.title: A Non-Inferiority Trial of 5-Day vs. 7-Day Antibiotic Therapy for Uncomplicated Community-Acquired Pneumonia in the Post-COVID-19 Era: Leveraging Electronic Phenotyping for Patient Selection
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare systems and highlighted the need for efficient, evidence-based care. For common infections like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), a seven-day antibiotic course is often a default, but shorter durations could reduce antimicrobial resistance, adverse events, and healthcare costs. This is particularly relevant for community-based care, where optimizing therapy duration can improve patient safety and resource utilization.
proposals.hypothesis: For adult patients with uncomplicated CAP, accurately identified via electronic phenotyping, a 5-day course of guideline-recommended antibiotics will be non-inferior to a 7-day course in terms of clinical cure at 30 days.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, randomized, controlled non-inferiority trial in a large healthcare network. Patients will be identified using an electronic phenotyping algorithm for uncomplicated CAP, adapted from Ha's prior work, which incorporates vital signs, laboratory data (e.g., procalcitonin), imaging reports, and comorbidity codes to exclude immunocompromised or severely ill patients. Eligible patients will be randomized to 5 or 7 days of therapy (e.g., amoxicillin/clavulanate or respiratory fluoroquinolone). The primary outcome is clinical cure at 30-day follow-up, assessed via electronic health record review and structured phone surveys. A non-inferiority margin of 10% will be used.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence to challenge a default therapy duration for a common infection, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship guidelines. It will demonstrate the practical utility of electronic phenotyping for precise patient selection in clinical trials. The findings could lead to safer, shorter antibiotic regimens, reducing collateral damage to patients and the community microbiome.
proposals.novelty: While duration trials exist, this proposal uniquely integrates an electronic phenotyping tool, previously used for inpatient stewardship, to rigorously define 'uncomplicated' CAP in a community-onset setting for a pragmatic trial. It directly builds on the researcher's expertise in pneumonia phenotyping and community-based care during the pandemic.

proposals.title: An Adaptive Platform Trial to Simultaneously Validate Diagnostic Criteria and Optimize Antibiotic Duration for Community-Acquired Pneumonia in the Post-Pandemic Era
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted critical gaps in syndromic definitions for respiratory infections and the urgent need for pragmatic antimicrobial stewardship. Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) management relies on consensus diagnostic criteria that may not reflect real-world heterogeneity, potentially leading to over-treatment. Concurrently, optimal antibiotic duration for CAP remains debated, with shorter courses often desirable but under-validated in diverse community settings.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive platform trial simultaneously testing multiple diagnostic criteria against a composite clinical/microbiological endpoint and multiple antibiotic duration arms will efficiently identify the most accurate diagnostic pathway and the non-inferior, shortest effective therapy duration for CAP.
proposals.method: We will implement a multi-center, Bayesian adaptive platform trial enrolling patients presenting to community pharmacies or primary care with suspected CAP. Participants meeting one of several pre-specified diagnostic definitions (e.g., IDSA/ATS consensus, electronic phenotype, modified clinical criteria) will be randomized to different durations (e.g., 5, 7, 10 days) of a standard empiric regimen (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate). Primary outcomes are diagnostic accuracy (linkage to 30-day clinical failure or procalcitonin trajectory) and clinical cure/recurrence at 30 days. A Bayesian response-adaptive randomization algorithm will preferentially allocate patients to better-performing diagnostic-therapeutic pathways, with pre-defined futility and superiority stopping rules to prune inferior arms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, efficient framework for co-optimizing diagnosis and treatment, moving beyond siloed validation studies. It will deliver actionable, evidence-based diagnostic criteria and therapy duration for CAP directly applicable to community and pharmacy-based care, strengthening outpatient antimicrobial stewardship. The platform can be adapted for future respiratory syndromes.
