proposals.novelty: This integrates Dr. Ha's expertise in electronic phenotyping and community pharmacy engagement into an adaptive trial that actively challenges diagnostic consensus, rather than merely validating it, while solving a core therapy duration question—a dual-purpose design not previously applied to CAP in community settings.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Multi-Center Trial to Validate the Implementation and Outcomes of a 7-Day Antibiotic Protocol for Community-Acquired Pneumonia: A Clinical 'Plugfest' Approach
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted critical gaps in the real-world application of clinical guidelines, particularly for common infections like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP). While consensus definitions and short-course therapy (e.g., 7 days) are promoted for stewardship, their practical implementation across diverse community and hospital settings remains unvalidated. This creates uncertainty about generalizability and may lead to inconsistent patient outcomes and antimicrobial use.
proposals.hypothesis: A prospectively applied, consensus-derived 7-day antibiotic protocol for CAP can be implemented with high fidelity across diverse clinical sites, and this consistent application will result in non-inferior clinical cure rates and reduced antibiotic exposure compared to site-specific historical practices.
proposals.method: We will design a pragmatic, multi-center trial across a heterogeneous network of hospitals and affiliated clinics, including rural and community pharmacies engaged in post-discharge care. The intervention is the implementation of a standardized electronic health record (EHR) phenotype for CAP and an associated 7-day antibiotic order set. The primary outcome is a composite of clinical failure. Secondary outcomes include protocol fidelity (measured via EHR audit), total antibiotic days of therapy, and consistency of results across sites (using mixed-effects models). The study will employ broad, pragmatic inclusion criteria to reflect real-world patients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prospective, multi-center validation of a short-course CAP protocol's real-world feasibility and effectiveness, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship. It will generate a novel framework for testing clinical guidelines that measures implementation fidelity and generalizability as key outcomes, moving beyond efficacy in controlled settings.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting the 'plugfest' concept from software interoperability testing to clinical research, explicitly testing guideline robustness across varied healthcare 'vendors' (sites). It integrates Dr. Ha's expertise in electronic phenotyping, community pharmacy roles, and outcomes of antibiotic therapy into a unified pragmatic trial design.

proposals.title: A Multi-Agent System for Decentralized Antimicrobial Stewardship: A Prospective Trial Validating Protocol Fidelity for Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare systems, revealing vulnerabilities in consistent protocol adherence for syndromes like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP). Existing electronic phenotyping tools and pharmacy-based interventions operate in silos, lacking a cohesive, automated system to manage complex treatment pathways. A decentralized, agent-based approach could enhance protocol fidelity in resource-limited or fragmented care settings, such as rural communities, by integrating disparate data sources.
proposals.hypothesis: A decentralized multi-agent system, operating under partial observability and simulated real-world communication constraints, can execute a consensus CAP definition and 7-day therapy protocol with non-inferior clinical efficacy and superior protocol fidelity compared to standard human-led stewardship.
proposals.method: We will design a prospective, pragmatic trial in a community hospital network. The system will use specific, pre-2026 agent architectures: a diagnostic agent using a pre-trained electronic phenotype model for CAP, a treatment agent aligned with IDSA guidelines to recommend therapy (e.g., beta-lactam selection), and a monitoring/pharmacy agent tracking adherence and labs. Agents communicate via a structured messaging protocol (e.g., based on FIPA-ACL) under imposed constraints like message delay, noise, or misaligned incentives (e.g., an agent 'preferring' broader-spectrum therapy) to test robustness. Primary outcomes are clinical cure rate and a composite protocol fidelity score.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prospective validation of a multi-agent system for real-world antimicrobial stewardship, directly extending Dr. Ha's work on electronic phenotyping and pharmacy-based interventions. It will yield a novel framework for deploying consensus guidelines in decentralized care environments and generate metrics for evaluating the resilience of clinical AI communication.
proposals.novelty: While multi-agent systems exist in theory, their application to prospectively manage a live clinical trial with intentional communication faults to simulate real-world data integrity challenges is novel. This moves beyond decision support to active, decentralized protocol execution.

proposals.title: Prospective, Multi-Center Validation of a Consensus Definition and 7-Day Treatment Protocol for Community-Acquired Pneumonia in the Post-Pandemic Era
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted traditional care pathways for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), accelerating the use of electronic phenotyping for stewardship. However, consensus definitions for CAP requiring hospitalization and optimal treatment duration remain variably applied in practice. Validating a standardized definition and a shorter, 7-day antibiotic course is critical for improving patient outcomes and combating antimicrobial resistance in community settings, especially in rural and underserved areas where pharmacy-based care expanded during the pandemic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adult patients hospitalized with CAP, identified via a standardized electronic phenotype consensus definition and treated with a protocolized 7-day antibiotic course, will have non-inferior clinical cure rates at 30 days compared to historical controls managed with usual care (typically 8-10 days).
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, prospective, multi-center clinical trial. Adult inpatients will be identified in real-time using an electronic health record (EHR) algorithm based on the proposed consensus definition (e.g., radiographic evidence, vital signs, laboratory criteria, and clinician diagnosis). Enrolled patients will be treated with a site-specific empiric antibiotic regimen (e.g., beta-lactam plus macrolide) for a protocol-mandated 7-day total duration. The primary outcome is clinical cure at 30-day follow-up, assessed via structured phone interview and EHR review. Secondary outcomes include 30-day readmission, mortality, and Clostridioides difficile infection rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prospective, real-world evidence for the effectiveness of a consensus electronic phenotype for CAP coupled with short-course therapy. It will directly inform inpatient antimicrobial stewardship protocols by offering a validated, syndrome-specific intervention. The findings will strengthen the evidence base for shorter antibiotic durations, potentially reducing adverse drug events and resistance selection pressure.
proposals.novelty: While electronic phenotyping for CAP exists, its prospective validation as an enrollment tool for a management trial is novel. Furthermore, testing a bundled intervention (phenotype + duration) in the post-COVID landscape, which altered pneumonia epidemiology and care access, addresses a contemporary knowledge gap.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Multi-Domain Staging System (GN-BSI Stage) for Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections in Immunocompromised Hosts
proposals.motivation: Current definitions of 'complicated' gram-negative bloodstream infection (BSI) are binary and inconsistently applied, leading to heterogeneous patient populations in clinical studies and variable clinical management. This lack of granularity hampers antimicrobial stewardship efforts, particularly in immunocompromised patients where outcomes are poor and antibiotic choices like cefepime versus carbapenems are critical. A standardized, graded staging system is needed to stratify mortality risk and guide therapy duration and escalation.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-domain staging framework for gram-negative BSI, integrating clinical, microbiological, and host-immune parameters, will provide superior prognostic discrimination for clinical failure and mortality compared to existing binary definitions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult patients with gram-negative bacteremia (Enterobacterales, Pseudomonas aeruginosa) from 2018-2024 at a tertiary cancer center. Using electronic health record data and microbiological results, we will define and score candidate domains: source control (timing/adequacy), host status (oncologic/immunosuppression), pathogen virulence (resistance genotype/phenotype), and early clinical response (72-hour vitals/labs). A weighted scoring algorithm will be developed using multivariable logistic regression for 30-day mortality, with internal validation via bootstrapping. The final GN-BSI Stage (I-IV) will be compared to binary definitions for discrimination (c-statistic).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, oncology-inspired staging system for gram-negative BSI, providing a common language for clinical trials and stewardship programs. It will offer a practical tool to identify high-risk patients who may benefit from prolonged or combination therapy and low-risk patients suitable for shorter courses, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship interventions.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the structured, multi-parameter TNM oncology staging philosophy to infectious diseases, moving beyond binary complication labels. It integrates contemporary electronic phenotyping methods with detailed microbiological data (including resistance mechanisms) to create a prognostic composite score.

proposals.title: Economic and Clinical Impact of a 'Just-In-Time' Protocol for Early Intravenous-to-Oral Transition in Community-Acquired Pneumonia: A Stewardship-Driven Analysis
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare resources, highlighting the need for efficient care models. Prolonged intravenous antibiotic therapy for common infections like community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) consumes significant inpatient resources (bed-days, nursing time, supplies) and increases costs and risks like hospital-acquired infections. Antimicrobial stewardship programs, including electronic phenotyping tools, have sought to optimize therapy, but a systematic, economically framed approach to shortening IV duration is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a protocol that uses specific, validated clinical 'pull signals' (e.g., afebrile for 24 hours, improving leukocytosis, tolerating oral intake) to trigger an early IV-to-oral switch for uncomplicated CAP will significantly reduce direct healthcare costs without compromising clinical outcomes, analogous to reducing inventory holding time in Just-In-Time logistics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental, pre-post study using electronic health record data from a multi-hospital system. Adult inpatients with electronically phenotyped CAP (leveraging Dr. Ha's prior work) will be identified. The intervention is a stewardship protocol defining 'pull signals' for early oral transition. The primary economic outcome is total direct cost of the CAP episode. Clinical outcomes (30-day readmission, mortality, recurrence) will be safety endpoints. We will use multivariable regression and interrupted time series analysis to compare the pre-protocol and post-protocol periods, adjusting for severity using pneumonia severity indices.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a quantitative, economic justification for shorter IV antibiotic courses, moving stewardship beyond clinical efficacy to direct value-based care. It will deliver a validated, operational protocol for 'pull signal'-driven oral transition that can be integrated into electronic stewardship tools. The work will also demonstrate the utility of supply chain efficiency models in healthcare operations.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly adapting the Just-In-Time inventory management framework to antimicrobial therapy duration, treating inpatient resources as 'inventory' to be minimized. While early switch studies exist, this protocol is driven by defined 'pull signals' from patient physiology, making the transition dynamic and patient-specific, rather than based solely on a fixed calendar day.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Multi-Center Trial of Ultra-Short-Course Intravenous Therapy for Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bacteremia: Clinical Non-Inferiority and Economic Impact Analysis
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained hospital resources and highlighted the risks of prolonged inpatient stays. For uncomplicated infections like community-onset bacteremia from Enterobacterales, a 7-day intravenous (IV) course is standard, but evidence supporting this duration is limited. This dogma leads to unnecessary hospital days, increased healthcare costs, and patient exposure to IV line complications, representing a major target for antimicrobial stewardship and value-based care.
proposals.hypothesis: For immunocompetent patients with uncomplicated, source-controlled bacteremia due to cefepime-susceptible organisms, a protocol-defined ultra-short-course (3 days) of IV therapy followed by immediate oral transition is clinically non-inferior to a standard 7-day IV course and is significantly more cost-effective.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, randomized, open-label, multi-center non-inferiority trial. Adult patients with uncomplicated bacteremia (e.g., from urinary source) due to pre-specified organisms (E. coli, Klebsiella spp.) will be randomized to intervention (3-day IV cefepime or equivalent, then oral step-down) or control (7-day IV). The primary endpoint is clinical cure at 30 days. A concurrent, prospective economic analysis will capture direct (drugs, IV supplies, nursing time) and indirect (bed-days, complications like CLABSI) costs using time-driven activity-based costing, integrated with electronic health record data via an electronic phenotyping algorithm to automate cost variable extraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide high-level evidence to challenge the entrenched 7-day IV therapy standard, directly informing stewardship guidelines. It will deliver a robust, real-world cost-effectiveness model that hospitals and payers can use to justify protocol changes, potentially reducing length of stay and healthcare costs without compromising patient outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While shorter courses are explored for some infections, a definitive trial targeting uncomplicated Gram-negative bacteremia with a 3-day IV backbone is lacking. The deep integration of a pragmatic clinical trial with a granular, real-time economic analysis designed to capture the full system-wide cost savings of reduced hospitalization is a novel synergy to drive practice change.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Shorter Intravenous Duration and Early Oral Transition for Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bacteremia in Community Settings: A Model-Based Study
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare resources, highlighting the need for efficient, cost-effective care models. Antimicrobial stewardship programs promoting shorter intravenous (IV) therapy and earlier oral transition for uncomplicated bacteremia can reduce hospital stays and complications, but their economic impact in community and post-pandemic settings is not fully quantified. This analysis is crucial for informing stewardship guidelines and pharmacy-based care transitions.
proposals.hypothesis: For immunocompetent patients with uncomplicated Gram-negative bacteremia from common pathogens (e.g., E. coli, Klebsiella spp.), a clinical pathway mandating shorter-course IV therapy (e.g., 4-7 days) followed by oral transition is cost-saving compared to traditional longer-course IV regimens, without increasing clinical failure rates.
proposals.method: We will develop a decision-analytic Markov model comparing two strategies: standard longer IV therapy versus early oral transition. Model parameters will be derived from a systematic literature review (pre-2026) of trials on short-course therapy for bacteremia. Key inputs include clinical failure rates (from studies like Dr. Ha's prior work on cefepime), drug costs, hospitalization costs per day, and rates of IV-line complications. We will use a US healthcare payer perspective, a one-year time horizon, and conduct probabilistic sensitivity analysis to assess parameter uncertainty. The primary outcome is the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a robust economic model to support antimicrobial stewardship decisions for bacteremia management in community hospitals and outpatient settings. It will quantify the potential cost savings from optimized therapy duration, offering evidence for healthcare administrators and clinicians. The findings will directly extend Dr. Ha's work on electronic phenotyping and specific antibiotic therapies into the health economics domain.
proposals.novelty: While clinical trials of shorter therapy exist, few pre-2026 studies integrate this clinical data into a formal cost-effectiveness model focused on the community care continuum, linking inpatient stewardship to outpatient pharmacy transitions, a gap highlighted during the pandemic's push for decentralized care.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Agent-Based and Discrete-Event Simulation to Predict and Optimize Implementation of Community-Acquired Pneumonia Guidelines in Outpatient Settings
proposals.motivation: Despite clear guidelines for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) management, adherence remains suboptimal, contributing to antimicrobial resistance and poor outcomes. The COVID-19 pandemic further disrupted outpatient workflows and resource availability, creating new, context-specific barriers to guideline implementation. Current implementation science relies heavily on post-hoc descriptive studies, which are reactive and inefficient for designing effective, proactive strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: A computational simulation model that integrates data on clinic workflow, staff behavior, and resource constraints can accurately predict adherence rates to CAP guidelines under different implementation strategies, enabling the pre-emptive identification and mitigation of key failure points.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid simulation model combining agent-based modeling (for clinician and patient decision-making) with discrete-event simulation (for clinic workflow and resource scheduling). The model will be parameterized using data from electronic health records (leveraging electronic phenotyping for CAP cases), time-motion studies in community clinics, and surveys of pharmacist and provider incentives, informed by the researcher's prior work. We will use historical data for calibration and validation, then simulate the impact of strategies like clinical decision support integration, pharmacy-based interventions, and workflow redesign on predicted guideline adherence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, engineerable tool for implementation science, shifting the paradigm from descriptive barrier analysis to predictive, systems-based intervention design. It will produce a validated, generalizable modeling framework that can be adapted to other syndromes and settings, directly supporting antimicrobial stewardship efforts in the community.
proposals.novelty: While simulation is used in healthcare operations, its application to prospectively model the complex socio-technical barriers to clinical guideline implementation is novel. This approach moves beyond static checklists of barriers to create a dynamic, testable digital twin of a clinical microsystem for implementation planning.

proposals.title: Bridging the Implementation Gap: Applying an Environmental Policy Framework to Improve Adherence to Syndromic Antimicrobial Stewardship Guidelines in Community Settings
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted critical gaps between the development of evidence-based clinical guidelines and their consistent implementation across diverse healthcare settings, particularly in community and rural contexts. This is especially problematic for syndromic stewardship, such as for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), where inappropriate antibiotic use persists. Pre-2026, the failure to effectively implement guidelines contributes to antimicrobial resistance and poor patient outcomes, mirroring the long-standing 'implementation gap' in environmental policy between regulation creation and on-the-ground compliance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically applying a multi-level implementation science framework, adapted from environmental policy analysis, to a specific CAP stewardship guideline will identify modifiable barriers and enable the design of tailored interventions that significantly improve guideline adherence in community hospital and pharmacy settings compared to standard dissemination.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR) and the Expert Recommendations for Implementing Change (ERIC) taxonomy, tools used in policy implementation science, to the clinical context. The study will first conduct a mixed-methods barrier analysis at policy, organizational (hospital/pharmacy), and clinician/pharmacist levels regarding CAP guideline adherence. Using electronic phenotyping (as in the researcher's prior work) to establish baseline adherence, we will then co-design and pilot a multi-component intervention. This will include: 1) context-specific implementation toolkits for pharmacies/hospitals, 2) training of embedded clinical champion teams modeled on regulatory liaisons, and 3) integration of guideline prompts and audit feedback into existing EHR/ pharmacy systems. Adherence will be measured pre- and post-intervention using electronic data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, transferable methodology for treating clinical guideline rollout as a complex policy implementation challenge. It will yield a tested, multi-level intervention package to improve syndromic antibiotic stewardship in community settings, directly addressing a key gap identified during the pandemic. The findings will contribute to implementation science by demonstrating the utility of cross-disciplinary framework adaptation from environmental policy.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual and methodological analogy from environmental policy implementation to a clinical problem, moving beyond typical healthcare-focused implementation models. It uniquely integrates the researcher's expertise in electronic phenotyping and community pharmacy engagement with a structured, diagnostic framework from another field to address stewardship.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Implementation-Effectiveness Trial of a Relaxed Source Control Protocol for Sepsis in Community Settings: Clinical Outcomes and Barriers to Adoption
proposals.motivation: Sepsis management guidelines historically emphasize definitive source control, yet during the COVID-19 pandemic, resource constraints and infection control concerns often delayed or precluded invasive source identification procedures, especially in community and rural settings. This created a pragmatic, unstudied paradigm where many patients were managed successfully without confirmed source control. Rigorously evaluating the safety of this relaxed approach and understanding the barriers to its systematic implementation is critical for creating resilient, real-world sepsis protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: For patients with sepsis and a clinically presumed but unconfirmed source of infection, management without mandatory invasive source control procedures will result in non-inferior clinical outcomes (e.g., 30-day mortality, treatment failure) compared to a standard protocol requiring confirmation, and the primary barriers to adopting this relaxed protocol will be clinician cognitive biases and institutional resource limitations rather than evidence skepticism.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial across a network of community hospitals and affiliated clinics, comparing a relaxed source control protocol to standard care. Concurrently, we will employ a mixed-methods implementation science framework (e.g., RE-AIM) guided by electronic phenotyping algorithms (similar to prior work on pneumonia) to identify eligible patients and track outcomes. Quantitative data on adoption fidelity will be triangulated with qualitative data from surveys (informed by pharmacy-based immunization research) and focus groups with clinicians to map specific barriers (e.g., fear of bacteremia relapse with narrower-spectrum therapy like cefepime).
proposals.contribution: This work will generate high-level evidence on the safety of a pragmatic sepsis management strategy relevant to resource-variable settings. It will directly produce a barrier-analysis framework and a tailored implementation toolkit (e.g., decision aids, audit tools) to guide future guideline rollouts, moving beyond retrospective adherence studies to proactive implementation design.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely integrates a clinical effectiveness question within a prospective implementation science design from the outset, a fusion not commonly applied to sepsis source control. It leverages the investigator's experience in community-based research and electronic phenotyping to study a protocol born from pandemic-era adaptations, formally testing a widespread but unvalidated practice.

proposals.title: Barriers to Guideline Adherence for Gram-Negative Bacteremia in Immunocompromised Patients: A Mixed-Methods Study of Community and Academic Hospital Settings
proposals.motivation: Recent pre-2026 consensus recommendations, such as those for managing cefoxitin-non-susceptible Enterobacterales bacteremia in immunocompromised patients, aim to optimize antibiotic selection and improve outcomes. However, guideline implementation is often inconsistent, especially across diverse settings like community hospitals and rural pharmacies, which were critical access points during the COVID-19 pandemic. Understanding the specific barriers to adherence is essential for effective antimicrobial stewardship and preventing treatment failure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adherence to new consensus guidelines for Gram-negative bacteremia is significantly lower in community hospitals compared to academic centers, driven by distinct barriers related to resource availability, pharmacist involvement, and electronic health record (EHR) integration.
proposals.method: We will conduct a convergent mixed-methods study. First, a retrospective cohort analysis using electronic phenotyping algorithms (similar to prior work on pneumonia) will quantify guideline adherence for eligible bacteremia cases across a network of community and academic hospitals. Second, we will perform structured surveys and semi-structured interviews with pharmacists, infectious diseases physicians, and hospitalists from these sites, informed by survey methods used in pharmacy-based immunization studies. Thematic analysis of qualitative data will be integrated with quantitative adherence metrics to identify modifiable site-specific barriers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed map of implementation barriers for a critical antimicrobial stewardship guideline across a care continuum. It will generate actionable data for designing tailored intervention strategies, such as refined EHR clinical decision support or targeted education, to improve antibiotic prescribing in vulnerable immunocompromised populations.
proposals.novelty: This work uniquely integrates the researcher's expertise in bacteremia outcomes and pharmacy-based community health services to study guideline implementation, moving beyond efficacy studies to address the real-world translational gap in diverse clinical settings post-pandemic.

proposals.title: Causal Adversarial Networks for Generalizable Prediction of Complicated Bacteremia: A Multi-Center EHR Study
proposals.motivation: Predicting progression to complicated bacteremia (e.g., metastatic infection, septic shock) is critical for guiding antibiotic selection and diagnostic workup. However, models trained on multi-center electronic health record (EHR) data risk learning spurious correlations from site-specific care patterns (e.g., variable use of echocardiography or specific antibiotics like cefepime) rather than true pathophysiology. This limits generalizability and clinical utility across diverse settings, including community hospitals and post-pandemic care environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model incorporating causal inference and adversarial learning to explicitly penalize reliance on site-specific practice patterns will learn more stable, generalizable predictors of complicated bacteremia risk than standard predictive models.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal adversarial neural network (CANN) architecture. The primary predictor network will learn to estimate the risk of complication using patient-level features (demographics, comorbidities, microbiology, initial vitals/labs). An adversarial discriminator network will simultaneously attempt to predict the data source (hospital site) from the primary network's learned representations. Training will involve gradient reversal to force the primary network to develop representations invariant to site, thereby isolating causal risk factors. Model performance and feature importance will be validated on held-out sites and compared against standard XGBoost and neural network baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, generalizable risk stratification tool for bacteremia complications, directly supporting inpatient antimicrobial stewardship. Methodologically, it will demonstrate the application of causal adversarial learning to a high-stakes clinical prediction problem, providing a framework to de-bias EHR models for multi-center use.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training has been used for domain adaptation in imaging, its application to de-bias bacteremia outcome models from institutional practice artifacts is novel. This approach moves beyond standard risk adjustment to actively disentangle care patterns from biology.

proposals.title: A Seismic Risk Model for Bacteremia: Machine Learning to Predict Metastatic Complications from Initial Blood Culture Data
proposals.motivation: Bacteremia, particularly in immunocompromised patients, carries a high risk of metastatic complications like endocarditis or septic shock, which dramatically increase mortality and healthcare costs. Current risk stratification is often based on clinician gestalt or simple scores, lacking the granularity to leverage the rich, temporal data available in the electronic health record (EHR) immediately after a positive blood culture. An early, accurate predictive tool could guide more intensive diagnostic workups and targeted antimicrobial therapy, improving patient outcomes and stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models, adapted from the temporal and spatial forecasting principles of seismic aftershock prediction, can identify complex, non-linear patterns in early EHR data to accurately stratify patients by risk of developing complicated bacteremia following an initial positive blood culture.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using EHR data from patients with bacteremia (e.g., Enterobacterales). The 'mainshock' is defined as the first positive blood culture, with features including pathogen identity, initial vital signs, comorbidities, and early lab values (e.g., lactate). The 'aftershock' outcome is a composite of metastatic infection, persistent bacteremia, or septic shock within 7 days. We will train and compare several models, including gradient boosting machines (XGBoost) and a recurrent neural network (RNN) to capture temporal vital sign trends, using a held-out test set for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, EHR-integratable risk stratification tool for clinicians managing bacteremia, enabling syndrome-specific stewardship. It will also demonstrate the translational value of cross-disciplinary analogies, specifically from geophysics to medicine, for generating innovative predictive models in infectious diseases.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual adaptation of seismic forecasting frameworks to a clinical infectious disease problem, moving beyond traditional logistic regression. This approach explicitly models bacteremia as a temporal sequence of events with latent risk patterns, a paradigm not previously applied in this domain.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Machine Learning-Stratified Trial of Risk-Adapted Duration of Therapy for Gram-Negative Bacteremia: Validating a Consensus Framework in the Community Setting
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare systems and underscored the need for efficient, evidence-based management of serious infections like bacteremia. While consensus definitions for bacteremia management exist, they are applied uniformly, potentially leading to over-treatment in low-risk patients and under-treatment in high-risk ones. A personalized, risk-stratified approach is needed to optimize antibiotic duration, reduce failure, and support antimicrobial stewardship, particularly in community and pharmacy-adjacent care settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Stratifying patients with Gram-negative bacteremia by a prospectively validated machine learning model for complication risk and assigning shorter (7-day) therapy to low-risk patients will yield non-inferior clinical outcomes compared to longer courses in high-risk patients, thereby validating the utility of ML-guided, consensus-based therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, prospective trial enrolling patients with community-onset Gram-negative bacteremia. At enrollment, a pre-trained gradient boosting model (XGBoost), validated on retrospective electronic health record data for features like source control, comorbidities, and early clinical response, will stratify patients into high- and low-risk groups for complications. All patients receive initial therapy per consensus guidelines. Low-risk patients are then randomized to 7-day versus standard (e.g., 10-14 day) therapy, while high-risk patients receive standard duration. The primary outcome is a composite of 30-day mortality, relapse, or re-admission.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prospective evidence for integrating a validated ML risk-stratification tool directly into bacteremia treatment protocols. It will generate high-quality data on the safety of shorter therapy in low-risk patients, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship programs. The study also offers a novel trial design template for testing syndrome-specific, personalized antibiotic strategies.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely fuses prospective ML validation with a pragmatic clinical trial, moving beyond retrospective electronic phenotyping to actively test an ML model's clinical utility. It innovates by using the consensus framework not as a static rule but as a platform for personalized duration decisions based on dynamic risk assessment.

proposals.title: Machine Learning for Early Risk Stratification of Complicated Bacteremia in Community and Hospital Settings: An Electronic Health Record-Based Study
proposals.motivation: Complicated bacteremia, involving metastatic infection or prolonged bacteremia, is associated with significantly higher mortality, longer hospital stays, and greater healthcare costs. Early identification of patients at high risk for complications is critical for guiding more intensive diagnostic workups and tailoring antimicrobial therapy, but existing clinical prediction rules are limited. This need is amplified in the post-COVID-19 pandemic era, where shifts in healthcare delivery and patient vulnerability patterns may have altered risk profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on structured and unstructured data from electronic health records (EHRs) available within the first 48 hours of a bacteremia diagnosis can accurately stratify patients by their risk of developing complicated bacteremia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using EHR data from adult patients with community-onset bacteremia. The outcome is complicated bacteremia, defined per IDSA guidelines. We will engineer features from demographics, comorbidities, vital signs, laboratory results (including microbiology), medication orders (e.g., cefepime use), and clinical notes via natural language processing (NLP) for key terms. We will compare the performance of several pre-2026 ML algorithms, including logistic regression with regularization, random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), using temporal validation. Model interpretability will be assessed using SHAP values.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, EHR-embedded prediction tool to help clinicians identify bacteremia patients requiring more aggressive management early in their course. It will extend electronic phenotyping methods, like those used for pneumonia, to bacteremia and provide novel insights into the pre-2026 determinants of complication risk, including specific antibiotic exposures and host factors.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to predict bacteremia onset or mortality, its use for specifically predicting the transition to *complicated* bacteremia using comprehensive EHR data is novel. This approach integrates the researcher's expertise in syndrome-specific stewardship and outcomes of specific antibiotic regimens (e.g., cefepime) into a dynamic prediction model.

proposals.title: Preference-Aware Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Antimicrobial Strategy in Community-Acquired Infections
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare systems, highlighting the need for adaptive, resource-efficient clinical decision support, particularly in community and outpatient settings like pharmacies. In antimicrobial stewardship, optimizing therapy for conditions like community-acquired pneumonia or bacteremia involves complex trade-offs between efficacy, toxicity, cost, and logistical burden (e.g., IV vs. oral, monitoring needs). Current AI models often optimize for a single, static reward function, failing to capture the nuanced and evolving preferences of clinicians and care teams.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) system, whose high-level strategy selection is guided by a dynamic model of clinician preferences elicited via discrete choice experiments (DCEs), will generate more clinically acceptable and adaptable treatment policies for community-acquired infections than standard reinforcement learning or static protocols.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-level HRL framework using the Options Framework or a similar architecture. The high-level policy selects among therapeutic strategies (e.g., de-escalation timing, IV-to-oral switch criteria), while low-level policies tune specific parameters (e.g., dose, frequency). Clinician preferences will be modeled via a latent utility function, updated through periodic, targeted in-silico DCEs embedded in the clinical workflow. These DCEs will present trade-offs based on real patient states (e.g., efficacy risk vs. renal toxicity risk). The preference model will directly shape the reward function for the high-level policy, creating a closed-loop learning system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for aligning AI-driven clinical decision support with human expertise and values. It will yield a proof-of-concept adaptive system for antimicrobial strategy that is both outcome-effective and preference-aligned, directly addressing the need for pragmatic stewardship tools highlighted in recent work on electronic phenotyping and pharmacy-based care during the pandemic.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a continuously updated, quantitatively elicited preference model into the reward structure of a hierarchical RL agent for clinical decision-making is a novel synthesis of behavioral economics and machine learning. This moves beyond using static historical data or fixed guidelines to create a dynamic, collaborative human-AI system.

proposals.title: An Adaptive Platform Trial to Optimize Antibiotic Duration for Community-Acquired Gram-Negative Bacteremia in the Post-Pandemic Era
proposals.motivation: Determining the optimal duration of antibiotic therapy for common infections like community-acquired bacteremia is critical for antimicrobial stewardship, yet evidence is often limited to pairwise comparisons of fixed durations. The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted healthcare delivery and potentially altered patient risk profiles and community pathogen patterns, necessitating efficient, contemporary evidence generation. Adaptive platform trials, which can compare multiple interventions against a shared control and modify enrollment based on interim results, offer a powerful but underutilized method to rapidly answer this duration question.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive, multi-arm platform trial design will efficiently identify non-inferior antibiotic durations for stable patients with community-acquired Enterobacterales bacteremia, compared to a standard 7-day course, without increasing the composite risk of clinical failure or relapse.
proposals.method: We propose a randomized, controlled, Bayesian adaptive platform trial in community hospitals. Eligible adults with uncomplicated bacteremia (e.g., from E. coli, Klebsiella) will be randomized to a control arm (7 days) or multiple experimental arms (e.g., 5, 10, 14 days). The primary outcome is a composite of 30-day all-cause mortality, relapse, or re-admission. A Bayesian response-adaptive randomization algorithm (e.g., Thompson Sampling) will be used, updating allocation probabilities every 50-100 patients based on interim analyses of the primary outcome and safety. Futility and superiority stopping rules will be pre-specified. Electronic phenotyping, as in Dr. Ha's prior work, will aid in efficient patient identification and outcome ascertainment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for implementing adaptive trials in community hospital settings for common infectious diseases. It will generate high-quality evidence on optimal antibiotic duration for a prevalent syndrome, directly informing stewardship guidelines. The study will also demonstrate the integration of electronic phenotyping with adaptive trial logistics to enhance efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive designs are used in oncology and COVID-19 trials, their application to antibiotic duration for common bacterial infections in community hospitals is novel. This proposal uniquely merges Dr. Ha's expertise in electronic phenotyping of community-acquired infection and comparative bacteremia outcomes with an innovative trial methodology to address a core stewardship question.

proposals.title: Adapting and Validating an Electronic Phenotype for Community-Acquired Pneumonia in Global, Resource-Variable Settings: A Pragmatic Stewardship Tool
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic strained healthcare systems globally, exacerbating challenges in antimicrobial stewardship, especially in settings with limited access to diagnostics and oral antibiotic formulations. Existing electronic phenotyping tools, like those for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), are validated in high-resource contexts and rely on comprehensive lab and pharmacy data, which are often unavailable in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) or rural areas, limiting their global applicability and utility for stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: A modified electronic CAP phenotyping algorithm, using a minimal set of universally available clinical and administrative data elements, can maintain adequate diagnostic accuracy for syndrome-specific stewardship in diverse, resource-variable settings compared to culture-based or high-resource electronic reference standards.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study across a network of hospitals in Southeast Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa with variable diagnostic capabilities. Using the previously published CAP electronic phenotype as a scaffold, we will iteratively modify inclusion criteria (e.g., vital signs, basic imaging reports, ICD-10 codes) and exclusion rules (e.g., removing reliance on specific oral antibiotic dispensations) based on local data availability. Performance (sensitivity, specificity, PPV) will be validated against a physician-adjudicated clinical CAP diagnosis. We will employ logistic regression to identify the minimal data set that preserves PPV >80% for stewardship targeting.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, context-adapted electronic tool to enable syndrome-specific antimicrobial use monitoring and intervention in settings where traditional stewardship is logistically challenging. It will provide a framework for scaling digital stewardship interventions globally, directly addressing equity in infectious disease management post-pandemic.
proposals.novelty: While electronic phenotyping is established, its deliberate adaptation for low-data environments within antimicrobial stewardship is novel. This bridges the researcher's expertise in electronic CAP phenotyping and pharmacy-based community health delivery, extending it to a global health context neglected by prior algorithm development.

ideas.title: Challenge that stewardship primarily targets prescriber behavior
ideas.description: Investigate if patient demand and cultural expectations are the primary drivers of inappropriate antibiotic use.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Role of Antibiotic Stewardship in Promoting Appropriate Antibiotic Use

ideas.title: Economic and Clinical Validation of Ultra-Short-Course Therapy for Uncomplicated Infections
ideas.description: This research proposes a pragmatic, multi-center trial to determine if a defined, ultra-short-course (e.g., 3-5 days) of IV therapy followed by immediate oral transition is non-inferior to a standard 7-day IV course for clearly defined uncomplicated infections. Crucially, the trial will embed a comprehensive economic analysis that captures not only drug and administration costs but also the significant savings from dramatically reduced hospital length-of-stay, nursing time, and complications associated with IV access. The synergy lies in generating the high-quality clinical evidence needed to challenge the seven-day dogma while providing the robust, real-world cost-effectiveness data required to drive immediate changes in hospital protocols and reimbursement models.

ideas.title: Prospective, ML-stratified trial of consensus-driven bacteremia therapy
ideas.description: This study embeds a prospectively validated machine learning model for complication risk into a real-world clinical trial testing the consensus definition for bacteremia. At enrollment, patients are stratified by the ML model into high- and low-risk for complications. All patients are then managed according to the consensus framework, but the key intervention—duration of therapy—is assigned based on this risk stratification, comparing shorter (e.g., 7-day) therapy in low-risk patients to longer courses in high-risk patients. This design simultaneously validates the ML model's clinical utility and tests the hypothesis that therapy duration guided by objective risk prediction improves outcomes compared to a one-size-fits-all application of the consensus recommendations.

ideas.title: Precision risk-stratification and protocolized monitoring to enable safe outpatient management of select immunocompromised infections
ideas.description: This research program first develops and validates a multidimensional risk score to identify the subset of immunocompromised patients at lowest risk for complications from specific infections, moving beyond the broad 'immunocompromised' label. It then designs and tests a tightly defined, technology-supported monitoring protocol with explicit escalation thresholds in a pragmatic clinical trial. The ultimate goal is to generate an evidence-based, operational standard that safely expands outpatient management by replacing a blanket exclusion criterion with a precision medicine approach.

ideas.title: Economic impact of shorter therapy and oral transition
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from manufacturing and supply chain logistics—specifically Just-In-Time (JIT) inventory management—to the clinical problem of antibiotic therapy duration. The analogous JIT solution reduces costs by minimizing inventory holding time and transitioning materials only as needed. The adapted idea is to analyze the economic impact of a 'Just-In-Time' healthcare model for uncomplicated infections, where shorter IV therapy and an earlier, protocol-driven transition to oral medication are triggered by specific, validated clinical 'pull signals.' This approach aims to quantify the cost savings from reducing the 'inventory' of inpatient resources (bed-days, nursing labor, IV supplies) and minimizing system waste, while ensuring clinical outcomes are not compromised.

ideas.title: Risk-Stratified, Response-Guided Management for Infections with Presumed Source Control
ideas.description: This study evaluates a protocol for managing infections where source control is presumed but not definitively confirmed. Instead of universally classifying them as uncomplicated, patients enter a closely monitored pathway with standardized clinical and biomarker assessments over a 48-72 hour window. Failure to meet predefined clinical response criteria triggers immediate escalation to advanced imaging and/or surgical consultation to identify and address an occult source. This approach safely tests the hypothesis that some infections can be managed as uncomplicated while systematically mitigating the risk of missing a persistent, life-threatening focus.

ideas.title: Scale up to global, resource-variable settings
ideas.description: Modify the consensus for settings with limited access to diagnostics or oral antibiotics.
ideas.sourceTitle: Optimizing the Management of Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: Consensus Guidance Using a Modified Delphi Process

ideas.title: Developing a Graded, Multi-Domain Definition for Complicated Gram-Negative BSI Using an Oncology Staging Framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from oncology, where complex diseases are defined using multi-domain staging systems (e.g., TNM classification), to the problem of defining complicated gram-negative bloodstream infections (BSI). The analogous solution involves integrating discrete, prognostic clinical and microbiological data into a composite, graded definition. The adapted idea is to develop a complementary consensus definition that moves beyond a binary classification. By creating a framework that scores domains like source control adequacy, host immunocompetence, pathogen-specific risk factors (e.g., resistance genes), and clinical response within a critical time window, a staged severity score can be generated. This granular definition would directly inform and standardize clinical decisions regarding antibiotic therapy duration, escalation to combination regimens, and the pursuit of source control, thereby improving patient outcomes and antimicrobial stewardship.

ideas.title: Implementation Science for a Pragmatic Paradigm: Studying the Adoption of a Relaxed Source Control Requirement in Sepsis Management
ideas.description: This research program integrates a clinical trial with an implementation science framework to evaluate if infections with presumed but unconfirmed source control can be safely managed as uncomplicated. Crucially, it embeds a concurrent, mixed-methods study to identify and analyze the specific clinician, institutional, and systemic barriers to implementing this relaxed requirement across varied healthcare environments. This approach will not only generate evidence on clinical outcomes but also produce a tailored implementation toolkit to guide future guideline dissemination, addressing adherence challenges proactively rather than retrospectively.

ideas.title: Challenge fixed duration and agent count in infection therapy: A multi-agent, adaptive treatment scheduling model
ideas.description: This research challenges the universality of both the seven-day treatment duration and the fixed use of a single antimicrobial agent. It proposes a computational and clinical investigation into how therapy outcomes for uncomplicated infections vary when the number of active therapeutic agents (from one to several, including combination or sequential therapies) is dynamically adjusted. The core synergy is modeling treatment not as a fixed-duration monotherapy but as a multi-agent system where duration is a function of the number, type, and timing of interacting agents, aiming to identify non-inferior, minimal-duration regimens that are personalized based on pathogen and host factors.

ideas.title: Relax the requirement for definitive source control
ideas.description: Test if infections with presumed but unconfirmed source control can be treated as uncomplicated.
ideas.sourceTitle: Optimizing the Management of Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: Consensus Guidance Using a Modified Delphi Process

ideas.title: The Clinician vs. Pathogen Duel: A Game-Theoretic Framework to Determine the Necessity of Repeat Blood Cultures
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the simplified competitive duel from game theory with the clinical question of repeat blood culture necessity. It models bloodstream infection management as a strategic, iterative duel between the clinician (seeking to confirm clearance with minimal testing) and the pathogen population (capable of persistence or resurgence). By applying dynamic game models to real-world bacteremia patient data, the study aims to identify the specific conditions—such as pathogen type, initial antibiotic response, and host immunity—under which forgoing a repeat culture is a Nash equilibrium, representing a rationally optimal and safe strategy. This provides a principled, mathematical foundation for personalized diagnostic de-escalation, moving beyond population-level guidelines.

ideas.title: Develop stewardship metrics for non-acute care settings
ideas.description: Create and validate specific outcome measures for stewardship programs in clinics and nursing homes.

ideas.title: Dynamic Host-Environment Alliances in Infection Management
ideas.description: This research inverts the static classification of a patient as 'immunocompromised' or 'immunocompetent' by modeling the host-pathogen-therapy system as a dynamic, multi-agent game. Instead of fixed treatment algorithms based on a one-time immune status, the idea proposes that a host's defensive capacity forms temporary, context-specific alliances with certain drugs or supportive measures against a pathogen, while potentially competing with other interventions at different disease stages. The core investigation is to identify the rules governing these shifting alliances—such as how a specific immunodeficiency might temporarily ally with a bacteriostatic drug to contain an infection without eradication, permitting safe outpatient monitoring. This enables the development of adaptive clinical protocols that respond in real-time to the evolving cooperative and competitive interactions within the host environment, moving beyond rigid risk stratification.

ideas.title: Implement adaptive platform trials for therapy duration
ideas.description: Use an adaptive trial design to efficiently compare multiple antibiotic durations simultaneously.
ideas.sourceTitle: Optimizing the Management of Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: Consensus Guidance Using a Modified Delphi Process

ideas.title: Applying 'Watchful Waiting' from Oncology to Blood Culture Stewardship
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that repeat blood cultures are always necessary in complicated cases by drawing an analogy from oncology's 'active surveillance' protocol for low-risk prostate cancer. In that field, the solution was to replace routine, repeat invasive biopsies with a monitored protocol using specific clinical and biomarker triggers to guide the necessity of further intervention. Adapted to infectious diseases, the research would investigate if a similarly structured, trigger-based protocol—using initial pathogen identity, antimicrobial susceptibility, host biomarker trends, and clinical response—can safely identify patients for whom all scheduled repeat blood cultures can be forgone, reserving them only for cases meeting pre-defined criteria for suspected treatment failure or metastatic infection.

ideas.title: Challenge seven days as a universal duration
ideas.description: Explore if therapy shorter than seven days is non-inferior for clearly uncomplicated infections.
ideas.sourceTitle: Optimizing the Management of Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: Consensus Guidance Using a Modified Delphi Process

ideas.title: Dynamic Multi-Agent Decision Systems for Optimizing Repeat Diagnostic Testing
ideas.description: This research investigates the safety and efficacy of forgoing repeat blood cultures by modeling the decision-making process as a multi-agent system with a variable number of participants. Instead of a single fixed protocol, a dynamic ensemble of specialized agents (e.g., representing microbiology, pharmacology, patient physiology, and hospital epidemiology) collaboratively analyzes initial culture results, clinical trajectory, and host factors. The system's architecture—whether employing two agents for straightforward cases or many for complex, immunocompromised hosts—determines the confidence threshold for recommending or omitting a repeat test. This synergy allows for rigorous, personalized testing strategies that challenge the one-size-fits-all repeat culture dogma while formally quantifying decision robustness across varying levels of clinical uncertainty.

ideas.title: Prospective validation of consensus definition and management using a multi-center pragmatic trial framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from software engineering's interoperability testing ('plugfests') to the clinical research context. In that field, new technical standards are validated through large-scale, real-world testing across diverse vendors and environments to ensure robustness. The adapted research idea is to prospectively validate a clinical consensus definition and its recommended 7-day therapy by designing a multi-center, pragmatic trial that mirrors this approach. Multiple, diverse clinical sites would implement the protocol within their standard workflows with broad inclusion criteria, serving as a clinical 'bake-off.' The study would measure not only patient outcomes but, crucially, the fidelity of protocol application and the consistency of results across varied real-world settings, thereby testing the generalizability and practical utility of the consensus guidelines.

ideas.title: Decentralized Team Learning for Adaptive Treatment Under Source Control Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research fuses the clinical hypothesis about relaxing definitive source control requirements with the Dec-POMDP formalism to create a novel team-based AI/decision-support paradigm. It formalizes the treatment team (e.g., surgeon, intensivist, infectious disease specialist) as decentralized agents with partial, local observations about the likelihood of source control. The joint policy is learned to optimize patient outcomes by dynamically adapting antibiotic strategy and diagnostic aggressiveness based on the team's collective, evolving belief state about source control, rather than a binary confirmed/unconfirmed classification. This enables a principled, data-driven exploration of when to safely treat as 'uncomplicated' under uncertainty, with the team's actions and communications explicitly modeled to manage risk.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Aftershock Prediction Models for Bacteremia Complication Risk
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where ML models predict earthquake aftershocks from initial seismic data, to the medical problem of predicting complications from bacteremia. In the analogous field, models use features of a mainshock (magnitude, location, fault data) to forecast subsequent seismic risk. Adapted to bacteremia, the 'mainshock' is the initial positive blood culture, and the 'aftershocks' are metastatic infections, persistent bacteremia, or septic shock. The research would involve training ML algorithms (e.g., gradient boosting, neural networks) on rich, temporal EHR data—including patient comorbidities, initial pathogen identification, vital sign trajectories, and early laboratory markers—to identify complex, non-linear patterns that stratify patients into low, medium, and high risk for developing complicated bacteremia. This risk stratification would provide clinicians with an early, data-driven tool to tailor diagnostic workups and therapeutic interventions, potentially improving outcomes and resource utilization.

ideas.title: Guideline implementation and adherence studies
ideas.description: Study barriers to implementing these consensus recommendations in diverse clinical settings.
ideas.sourceTitle: Optimizing the Management of Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: Consensus Guidance Using a Modified Delphi Process

ideas.title: Causal Machine Learning for Generalizable Bacteremia Complication Risk Stratification
ideas.description: This research develops a risk stratification model that combines machine learning with causal inference methodologies to predict progression to complicated bacteremia. The system is explicitly designed to disentangle true clinical risk factors from institutional care patterns and surveillance biases present in multi-center electronic health record data. By learning stable, causal relationships, the model aims to produce robust predictions that generalize across diverse hospital settings and patient populations. The final output is a validated tool that supports clinical decision-making by identifying high-risk patients based on pathophysiology, not local practice artifacts.

ideas.title: Adapting Implementation Science Frameworks from Environmental Policy to Clinical Guideline Rollout
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from the field of Environmental Policy and Regulation, which has a long history of studying the gap between the creation of standards (e.g., for pollution control) and their practical implementation across varied regions and industries, this research adapts its solution. The analogous solution involves using structured, multi-level implementation science frameworks to diagnose barriers at systemic, organizational, and individual levels, followed by deploying tailored strategies like pilot programs, feedback mechanisms, and incentive structures. Adapted to the clinical context, the research idea is to systematically apply these frameworks to the problem of clinical guideline implementation. The study would map the translation pathway of a specific set of consensus recommendations, identifying analogous barriers at the health system (policy/funding), hospital/clinic (workflow/culture), and clinician (knowledge/attitude) levels. It would then design, pilot, and evaluate a suite of interventions—such as context-specific implementation toolkits, embedded clinical champions modeled on regulatory liaisons, and electronic health record integrations acting as compliance feedback systems—to improve adherence in diverse clinical settings, effectively treating guideline rollout as a complex policy implementation challenge.

ideas.title: Multi-Agent Ensemble Learning for Dynamic, Patient-Specific Bacteremia Complication Risk Modeling
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel machine learning framework for bacteremia complication risk stratification that moves beyond a single predictive model. Instead, it employs a variable ensemble of specialized ML 'agents,' where each agent is an algorithm trained to excel on specific patient subpopulations, data modalities (e.g., vitals, lab trends, comorbidities), or hypothesized complication pathways. For each new patient, the system dynamically assembles a tailored team of relevant agents—from a minimal set for straightforward cases to a large, diverse committee for complex presentations—whose predictions are aggregated. This approach mimics clinical reasoning by incorporating multiple perspectives and allows the model's complexity to adapt to the uncertainty and heterogeneity of the individual case, potentially improving both accuracy and clinical interpretability.

ideas.title: Predictive Systems Modeling for Proactive Guideline Implementation
ideas.description: This research develops and validates computational simulation models of clinical microsystems (e.g., outpatient clinics, hospital wards) to prospectively identify and overcome barriers to guideline adherence. By integrating data on workflow, resources, incentives, and team dynamics, the models simulate the impact of different implementation strategies on adherence outcomes before any real-world change is attempted. This allows for the iterative, low-cost redesign of implementation plans to preemptively address predicted failure points, moving the field from descriptive barrier studies to engineered, context-sensitive solutions.

ideas.title: Adapting Precision Agriculture's Stress-Tolerant Crop Monitoring to Immunocompromised Host Management
ideas.description: This idea inverts the host immune status criterion by challenging the assumption that immunocompromised automatically equals high-risk/complicated management. Drawing an analogy from precision agriculture, where stress-tolerant crops are identified through continuous monitoring and managed with optimized support instead of blanket intensive protocols, this research adapts that solution. It proposes to develop a precision monitoring framework for immunocompromised hosts (e.g., with well-controlled HIV or specific drug-induced immunosuppression) using frequent biomarker assays, wearable devices, and digital symptom tracking. The goal is to identify a sub-population that demonstrates clinical stability and 'managed tolerance,' enabling their safe treatment under a streamlined, 'uncomplicated' management pathway with close, data-driven oversight, thereby personalizing care and potentially reducing unnecessary medical escalation and resource use.

ideas.title: Relax the assumption that appropriateness is always definable
ideas.description: Study scenarios with diagnostic uncertainty where 'appropriate use' is ambiguous and stewardship must guide probabilistic treatment.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Role of Antibiotic Stewardship in Promoting Appropriate Antibiotic Use

ideas.title: Multi-Agent Modeling for Dynamic, Cost-Effective Clinical Pathway Optimization
ideas.description: This research proposes developing a multi-agent simulation framework where intelligent agents explicitly model the policies, incentives, and likely decisions of key stakeholders in a healthcare system (e.g., patients, physicians, hospital administrators, insurers). This system is applied to the specific clinical scenario of transitioning from IV to oral therapy, where agents, through their interactions and modeled reasoning, dynamically identify and validate personalized, cost-saving pathways. The novel synergy lies in using opponent/teammate modeling to predict and influence real-world adoption barriers and behavioral responses, thereby generating not just a static economic analysis but a dynamic, adaptive policy tool that maximizes both economic savings and protocol adherence.

ideas.title: Preference-Aware Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Clinical Strategy
ideas.description: This research synthesizes hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) and discrete choice experiments (DCEs) to develop an adaptive AI for clinical decision support. The HRL framework separates high-level therapeutic strategy selection from low-level parameter tuning (e.g., dosage timing). The system's high-level policy is informed by a continuously updated model of clinician preferences, which is quantitatively elicited through targeted, in-silico DCEs that present trade-offs between outcomes, burdens, and risks. This creates a closed-loop where the AI learns not just from patient outcomes but also from aligned clinical expertise, resulting in strategies that are both effective and tailored to the evolving value judgments of the care team.

ideas.title: Validating Clinical Consensus via Multi-Agent Communication Under Partial Observability
ideas.description: This research designs a real-world clinical trial where the application of a consensus definition and 7-day therapy protocol is managed not by a single human team, but by a decentralized network of autonomous or semi-autonomous software agents. Each agent has a partial view of the patient's state (e.g., lab results, symptoms, adherence) and must communicate with other agents (e.g., diagnostic, treatment, monitoring agents) under constraints that include potential deception or misaligned incentives, simulating real-world data integrity challenges. The trial prospectively validates whether the consensus protocol can be reliably executed in this complex environment, while the primary outcome evaluates both clinical efficacy and the success of the inter-agent communication protocols in maintaining protocol fidelity.

ideas.title: Emergent Diagnostic Coordination in Partially Observable Epidemics
ideas.description: This research proposes a multi-agent simulation where simple agents, governed by minimalist rules, must classify and manage simulated infection cases based on local, noisy, and incomplete data about source control. The core investigation is whether emergent coordination among these agents can lead to system-level treatment protocols that are as effective as those requiring confirmed source attribution, effectively 'relaxing' the informational requirement through collective intelligence. This tests a hypothesis in both AI and epidemiology: that complexity in diagnostic strategy can emerge from simple interactions under uncertainty, rather than from complex, centralized logic. The outcome could inform both the design of resilient public health systems and decentralized AI decision-making architectures.

ideas.title: Adaptive Platform Trial for Diagnostic Validation and Therapy Optimization
ideas.description: This study employs an adaptive platform trial design to simultaneously challenge the consensus definition and the standard therapy duration. It prospectively enrolls patients meeting either the consensus criteria or one of several pre-specified alternative diagnostic definitions, randomizing them across multiple therapeutic duration arms. The primary outcomes are a composite of diagnostic accuracy (linking each definition to a hard clinical or biomarker endpoint) and comparative clinical effectiveness of therapy durations. The adaptive design allows for the early discontinuation of inferior diagnostic pathways or therapy durations, ensuring the study actively identifies the best practice rather than merely validating a pre-existing consensus.

ideas.title: Adapting Computational Drug Design for Inverse Pommerman Level Generation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse problem from computational drug design to the domain of environment design for agent testing. In drug design, the goal is to generate molecules that bind to a specific biological target; here, the goal is to generate Pommerman levels that 'bind to' or rigorously test a specific agent capability. The analogous solution involves defining a quantitative scoring function that measures how much a given level challenges a target capability (e.g., cooperation, item use, or long-term planning). Using this as a fitness function, a generative algorithm (such as a quality-diversity algorithm or a conditional GAN) would explore the space of possible level layouts, enemy AI behaviors, and item distributions. The output is a suite of automatically generated levels that are 'optimized' to be diagnostic tests for the pre-specified capability, creating a scalable, targeted benchmark for evaluating and improving intelligent agents.

ideas.title: Adapting Cognitive Behavioral Models for Multi-Agent Intention Inference
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from clinical psychology to the problem of opponent and teammate modeling. In Cognitive Behavioral Therapy, practitioners construct and iteratively test models of a patient's internal cognitive schemas to predict behavior. Analogously, we propose building agents that explicitly generate and maintain structured cognitive models (e.g., belief trees, goal hierarchies, heuristic rules) for other players. These models are treated not as static estimates but as dynamic hypotheses. The agent would then design targeted interactions—such as specific moves, feints, or structured communication—to actively gather evidence, disambiguate intentions, and refine its models, leading to more robust and explainable modeling of others' policies in cooperative or competitive multi-agent settings.

ideas.title: Dec-POMDP-Based Learning for Emergent Team Conventions in Pommerman
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the Dec-POMDP framework with the Pommerman team environment to investigate the emergence of robust team conventions. The core idea is to structure the Dec-POMDP's joint policy learning to incentivize the discovery of shared, interpretable protocols for communication and coordination, such as bomb-based signaling or dynamic role switching, under the constraints of partial observability and active opposition. This moves beyond pure policy optimization to study how effective and transferable team-level abstractions can be formed from decentralized experience. The resulting framework provides a testbed for analyzing convention formation, robustness to teammate uncertainty, and the transfer of learned conventions to new partners or scenarios within Pommerman.

ideas.title: Comparative Phylogenetic Analysis of Competition-Winning AI Architectures
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodology of phylogenetic analysis from evolutionary biology to the domain of AI competition analysis. The analogous problem is understanding how successful traits emerge and relate across different lineages. The analogous solution involves constructing phylogenetic trees based on comparative trait analysis to reveal evolutionary history and adaptive innovations. The adapted idea is to host the NIPS 2018 competition and then perform a detailed phylogenetic-style analysis on the successful agent strategies and architectures. By defining a set of computational 'traits' (e.g., model family, attention mechanisms, reward shaping techniques), we can construct a 'phylogeny' of the submitted solutions. This will systematically uncover the 'family trees' of successful approaches, distinguish between truly novel innovations and incremental variations, and identify which architectural or strategic 'adaptations' were most critical for success in the competition's environment, providing a principled framework for understanding progress in the field.

ideas.title: Continuous-Time Multi-Agent Modeling with Asynchronous Belief Propagation
ideas.description: This research direction investigates multi-agent systems where agents operate in a continuous or asynchronous time environment, removing the turn-based assumption. Crucially, each agent employs explicit opponent and teammate modeling techniques, but these models are updated dynamically as observations stream in, without waiting for a discrete turn cycle. This creates a synergy where an agent's belief about another agent's strategy evolves in real-time, allowing for predictions about immediate future actions and more nuanced, temporally-precise counter-strategies. The core challenge and novelty lie in developing efficient algorithms for propagating belief updates and policy inferences across agents operating on different time scales and with partial, staggered observability.

ideas.title: Capability-Centric Diagnostic Environment Generation via Interpretable Skill Decomposition
ideas.description: This system generates environments that provide rigorous, interpretable diagnostics of specific agent skills. It first profiles a target agent population within a base environment to decompose their behavior into a vector of fundamental capabilities using trajectory analysis and concept activation. A generator then creates environments optimized to maximally stress individual capabilities or specific combinations, validated for generalization across randomized level variants and agent architectures. The output is a suite of levels paired with explicit capability diagnoses, enabling targeted agent improvement and robust benchmarking.

ideas.title: Adapt Swarm Intelligence Consensus Algorithms for Dec-POMDP Policy Learning
ideas.description: The original idea formalizes multi-agent team coordination as a Dec-POMDP for joint policy learning. Drawing an analogy from swarm robotics and collective animal behavior, where agents with partial observability achieve global coordination through local interaction rules and consensus algorithms (e.g., averaging neighbor states). The adapted solution is to integrate swarm intelligence consensus mechanisms into Dec-POMDP policy learning. Specifically, design algorithms where agents, during training or execution, share and iteratively average policy parameters or value estimates with neighbors defined by a communication network. This decentralized consensus approach enables scalable and robust learning of joint policies without full centralization, directly translating the swarm's emergent coordination solution to the Dec-POMDP framework.
