ideas.title: Theory of Mind-Driven Environment Generation for Agent Capability Profiling
ideas.description: This research direction integrates cognitive modeling for teammate belief inference (Theory of Mind) with automated environment generation. The core idea is to design an algorithm that, by observing an agent's behavior and constructing a model of its inferred beliefs and intentions, can then generate tailored Pommerman levels that probe the accuracy and robustness of that specific cognitive model. This creates a novel paradigm for evaluating and improving multi-agent reasoning, where environments are not just tests of general skill but are actively engineered to diagnose specific gaps in an agent's social understanding. The synergy enables automated, curriculum-based training and benchmarking for Theory of Mind capabilities in complex, partially observable settings.

ideas.title: Learning Emergent Communication Protocols in Dec-POMDPs with Adversarial Perturbations
ideas.description: This research formalizes multi-agent communication under partial observability and potential deception within the Dec-POMDP framework, treating the communication protocol itself as a learnable component of the joint policy. The core innovation is to introduce adversarial perturbations or deceptive agents into the training environment, forcing the emergent communication protocols to develop robustness and contextual grounding that pure cooperation or isolated protocol testing cannot achieve. The work will develop algorithms to learn these resilient protocols and provide theoretical analysis on the conditions under which useful, deception-resistant communication emerges as a component of the optimal joint policy.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Robust Transfer in Monte Carlo Tree Search
ideas.description: This system enhances Transfer MCTS with a meta-learning layer that actively assesses and mitigates the risk of negative transfer. It learns to predict when and where transferred value functions or rollout policies are reliable, dynamically blending them with online learning within the MCTS tree. The approach includes a mechanism for task-conditioned uncertainty estimation to guide exploration away from regions where prior knowledge is potentially misleading. This results in a robust planning agent that safely leverages past experience to reduce sample complexity while remaining resilient to domain shifts inherent in large, partially observable problems.

ideas.title: Resource-Bounded Pragmatic Modeling with Deception-Robust Fallbacks
ideas.description: This system builds agents that strategically model others only when computationally justified and the situational stakes are high, avoiding constant overhead. It uses meta-learning to quickly identify deceptive or inconsistent opponents and switch to robust, model-free counter-strategies. The architecture explicitly manages uncertainty about other agents' models, allowing the agent to gracefully degrade performance rather than fail catastrophically. This results in a resilient agent that gains the benefits of modeling when possible but remains effective and efficient in adversarial or computationally constrained settings.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery for Latent Game Mechanics
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from the field of Computational Biology, specifically Gene Regulatory Network Inference, where algorithms must discover causal interaction rules (which genes regulate others) from noisy observational data without a known model. The analogous solution uses causal discovery algorithms to construct a directed graph of influences by systematically testing for conditional independencies and leveraging interventions. Adapted to the RL context, the idea is to train agents that do not assume known game rules. Instead, the agent must actively discover the latent causal mechanics of the environment (like bomb blast range, item functions, or trigger conditions) by building and refining a causal graphical model from its interaction history. It uses its actions as targeted interventions to test causal hypotheses, enabling it to distinguish spurious correlations from true game mechanics and ultimately learn a compact, interpretable model of the environment's underlying rules.

ideas.title: Automated Curriculum Generation for Rule-Discovery Agents
ideas.description: This research direction combines the challenge of learning in environments with unknown rules (like bomb physics) with automated level generation. The core idea is to develop a system where a generator creates Pommerman levels not just for difficulty, but to systematically test and train an agent's rule-hypothesis formation and experimentation strategies. This creates an adaptive curriculum that progressively exposes the agent to scenarios requiring the discovery of specific, hidden mechanics. The synergy lies in using the generator as an automated teacher that shapes the agent's learning process for meta-cognitive skills, rather than just final performance.

ideas.title: Causal Stress-Testing and Generalization Analysis of Competitive RL Agents via Adversarial Environment Generation
ideas.description: This project first replicates the NIPS 2018 competition to establish baseline winning agents. It then moves beyond post-hoc analysis by deploying an automated framework that generates a diverse spectrum of environment modifications, including distribution shifts, altered dynamics, and adversarial perturbations. By systematically evaluating the performance degradation and adaptation of the winning strategies across this expanded test suite, the research identifies the causal factors behind their success and measures their true robustness. The output is a set of falsifiable principles about agent design that are validated for generalizability, not just a report on a historical contest.

ideas.title: Grounding Emergent Communication: Protocols Tested for Generalization, Semantics, and Causal Influence
ideas.description: This research designs and evaluates multi-agent communication protocols under partial observability with a core focus on preventing degenerate, non-generalizable solutions. The system trains agents on a suite of cooperative tasks requiring communication but evaluates them using a strict three-tier protocol: transfer to unseen partners and task variants, performance on external referential games requiring alignment with human-interpretable concepts, and causal mediation analysis to quantify the actual information content of messages. This approach ensures developed protocols are robust, semantically grounded, and resistant to deceptive or opaque coordination strategies that score well on training but fail fundamentally as communication.

ideas.title: Transfer Monte Carlo Tree Search via Hierarchical Abstract Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational protein design, where hierarchical abstraction is used to manage vast combinatorial spaces. The analogous solution uses coarse-grained models to guide detailed atomic searches. Adapted to the original challenge of MCTS for planning with large branching factors and partial observability, the research proposes a Transfer MCTS framework that learns and leverages abstract models. A high-level MCTS planner explores a simplified, abstracted version of the problem to find strategic outlines. This abstract policy or value function is then transferred to guide and prune the search tree of a low-level MCTS that operates on the full, complex observation space, enabling efficient planning by transferring strategic knowledge across levels of abstraction.

ideas.title: Adapting Statistical Ensemble Methods to Multi-Agent System Scaling
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the fixed four-agent player count common in many multi-agent system studies. By drawing an analogy from statistical mechanics, where systems are understood by analyzing their behavior across different scales (from few particles to the thermodynamic limit), we propose to investigate multi-agent dynamics with a variable number of agents (N). The analogous solution involves using ensemble methods and scaling laws to find universal principles. Adapted to this context, the research will systematically explore the 'phase diagram' of agent interactions, identifying critical thresholds and regime shifts (e.g., from negotiation to market-like or crowd dynamics) as N increases from 2 to many. The outcome aims to develop a foundational framework for scalable, N-agnostic agent interaction models, moving beyond designs optimized for a single, arbitrary group size.

ideas.title: Causally Consistent Multi-Agent Learning with Bounded Temporal Asynchrony
ideas.description: This research designs a multi-agent system where agents act and learn in a continuous-time environment, but their decision-making is regulated by a lightweight synchronization protocol that enforces causal message ordering and a guaranteed maximum staleness bound (Δ) for observations. This approach prevents the incoherence of pure asynchrony by ensuring agents never act on arbitrarily outdated worldviews, enabling stable coordination. The core technical challenge is developing temporally-extended value functions and policies that are robust to predictable, bounded delays within the Δ window. The system is evaluated on benchmarks requiring tight coordination under real-time pressure, measuring both performance and the deviation from ideal synchronous baselines.

ideas.title: Bayesian Inverse Planning for Teammate Mental State Inference
ideas.description: This research adapts the Inverse Optimal Control (IOC) solution from cognitive neuroscience and motor control—where an observer infers an agent's hidden goals or cost functions from their physical movements—to the problem of computational theory of mind for teammate modeling. The analogous solution assumes the observed agent is a bounded-rational planner, and inverts this generative model using Bayesian inference. The adapted idea applies this framework to AI agents: by observing a teammate's action sequences within a shared, partially observable environment, the modeling agent performs Bayesian inverse planning. This process infers the teammate's latent reward function and subjective beliefs about the world state, creating a rich cognitive model that predicts future behavior based on inferred intentions and goals, rather than just action patterns.

ideas.title: Active Probing for Robust Teammate Model Inference Under Uncertainty and Deception
ideas.description: This system addresses the brittleness of standard theory-of-mind modeling by combining probabilistic inference over a diverse space of potential teammate models (rational, heuristic, adversarial, or learned) with targeted, information-gathering actions. The agent actively plans interventions or probes to reduce uncertainty about the teammate's true decision-making process, distinguishing between cooperation, incompetence, and deception. This enables robust collaboration even with unfamiliar, non-stationary, or partially adversarial teammates by continuously testing and updating its beliefs, moving from passive observation to active model discrimination.

ideas.title: Event-Driven Hierarchical RL with Meta-Controller Interrupts for Dynamic Strategy Adaptation
ideas.description: This system employs a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework where a high-level meta-controller learns to select and monitor flexible temporal sub-policies (strategies). Crucially, it integrates a learned interruption mechanism based on real-time event detection, allowing the meta-controller to abort a failing sub-policy and re-plan immediately upon encountering unexpected game events. This combines the long-term coherence of planning with the agility of reactive control, ensuring the agent can dynamically shift between navigation, placement, and defensive tactics. The result is a robust agent that maintains strategic direction while being highly responsive to tactical surprises.

ideas.title: Hypothesis-Driven Exploration with Separated Discovery and Policy Phases
ideas.description: This approach trains agents to actively form and update internal probabilistic models of hidden game mechanics, such as bomb physics, through deliberate experimentation. A meta-controller allocates safe exploration budgets to test specific hypotheses in controlled settings, minimizing catastrophic failures. The knowledge gained from this structured discovery phase is then distilled into a robust world model, which is subsequently used for training a separate, goal-oriented policy. This separation ensures efficient learning and prevents the policy from being derailed by the inherently risky process of rule discovery.

ideas.title: Scalable Regimes: Discovering Phase Transitions and Scaling Laws in Variable-Population Multi-Agent Systems
ideas.description: This research moves beyond description to establish a predictive theory for how multi-agent system dynamics change with population size. It posits that key interactive properties undergo phase transitions or follow scaling laws as a function of agent count and environmental constraints. Through controlled experiments across a spectrum of agent numbers, the work will map these regimes, identify critical thresholds, and derive principles that explain and predict emergent behavior for any given scale, providing a foundational framework for scalable multi-agent design.

ideas.title: The Embedded Duel: Pairwise Competition within a Dynamic Multi-Agent Field
ideas.description: This research program studies the foundational one-versus-one competitive scenario not in isolation, but as a component embedded within a richer multi-agent environment. The core methodology involves running iterative two-player duels while varying external parameters such as audience effects, the availability of third-party alliances, and resource flows from a broader ecosystem. The goal is to develop a more robust theory of direct competition that accounts for how pairwise strategies evolve and are exploited when the duel does not occur in a vacuum, thereby bridging the gap between simplified models and real-world strategic complexity.

ideas.title: Generating Dynamic Alliance Environments to Stress-Test Agent Diplomacy and Adaptation
ideas.description: This research direction develops an inverse environment generation system for multi-agent arenas like Pommerman, where the core objective is to automatically produce levels that specifically challenge an agent's capacity for dynamic alliance formation and betrayal. The generator controls spatial elements, item placement, and, crucially, rule-based or event-driven triggers that incentivize or force shifts between cooperation and competition within a single episode. This enables the creation of a benchmark suite for 'diplomatic intelligence,' testing agents on capabilities such as trust modeling, commitment breaking, and opportunistic coordination that are impossible to evaluate in static team structures.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Multi-Resolution Navigation for Infinite Procedural Maps
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from cosmology and astrophysical simulation—specifically Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR)—to the problem of testing agent generalization on much larger or infinite procedurally generated maps. The analogous problem in cosmology is simulating the effectively infinite universe across vastly different scales, from galaxies to the cosmic web, which is solved by using hierarchical models and dynamically adjusting computational resolution only where needed. The adapted idea proposes developing agents with hierarchical, multi-resolution spatial representations. The agent would learn to maintain a persistent, coarse topological graph of the global environment (the 'cosmic web' of the map) while using procedural generation to create high-fidelity, local 'patches' (like refined simulation meshes) on-demand for navigation and interaction. This separation of scales allows the agent to reason about long-range travel and global structure efficiently while retaining the ability to perform precise local tasks, directly testing and enabling generalization across orders of magnitude in map size.

ideas.title: Adapting Asynchronous Distributed Consensus for Real-Time Multi-Agent Decision-Making
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the turn-based, synchronous time assumption in multi-agent systems by drawing an analogy from distributed computing. The analogous problem in Distributed Systems is achieving reliable consensus among independent, fault-prone nodes without synchronous communication, solved by protocols like Paxos. The adapted idea applies this solution to multi-agent decision-making: agents act as nodes in an asynchronous network, proposing actions and voting in real-time based on locally perceived states and received messages. The system uses a quorum-based commitment mechanism to finalize joint decisions, enabling coherent, emergent coordination without a global turn-taking clock, thus creating robust, real-time collaborative agents that can handle dynamic environments and unreliable communication.

ideas.title: Adapting Swarm Intelligence for Cooperative-Competitive Multi-Agent Pommerman
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from swarm robotics and collective animal behavior to the team-based Pommerman problem. The analogous field solves coordination in large, decentralized groups using simple local interaction rules (e.g., stigmergy, flocking rules) to produce robust, emergent collective intelligence. The adapted idea is to create Pommerman agents that operate on similar principles. Each agent would be programmed with rules governing movement, bomb placement, and item use based on locally observable states of allies and enemies, promoting emergent team tactics like coordinated pushes, defensive formations, and opportunistic attacks. This approach prioritizes robustness and adaptability over pre-scripted team plays or dense communication, mimicking how natural swarms effectively cooperate in competitive environments.

ideas.title: Use cellular automata for neural activation rules

ideas.title: Hierarchical Role-Based Learning for Scalable Multi-Agent Teams under Partial Observability
ideas.description: This approach introduces a hierarchical Dec-POMDP framework that decomposes the joint policy learning problem into manageable layers. At a higher level, a meta-policy or learned role allocator assigns dynamic sub-tasks or roles to agents based on team state. At a lower level, agents learn role-specific policies conditioned on these assignments and limited local observations, using factored value functions or structured communication to approximate coordination. This hierarchical abstraction drastically reduces the joint action-observation space, enabling scalable learning via deep reinforcement learning while preserving theoretical grounding in decentralized decision-making under partial observability.

ideas.title: Deep Meta-Strategy Search for Robust Policy Learning in Stochastic Games
ideas.description: This approach addresses equilibrium intractability by combining multi-agent reinforcement learning with empirical game-theoretic analysis. A diverse population of opponent strategies is maintained and evolved through self-play, creating a meta-game over these strategies. A protagonist agent is then trained not against a single equilibrium, but to approximate a robust meta-strategy that minimizes regret against the current population distribution. This results in a computationally feasible method that learns policies with strong robustness guarantees in complex environments without requiring explicit equilibrium computation.

ideas.title: Dynamic Alliance Formation in Mechanically Opaque Multi-Agent Games
ideas.description: This research direction trains multi-agent systems in environments where both the core game mechanics (e.g., damage rules, object interactions) and the cooperative-competitive structure are hidden and must be discovered through interaction. Agents cannot rely on predefined teams; instead, alliances must be formed and broken dynamically based on continuously evolving, agent-specific hypotheses about the underlying game rules. This forces the development of agents that engage in theory-of-mind reasoning and strategic communication to test their mechanistic beliefs and negotiate temporary partnerships, as the utility of any alliance depends on a currently unknown world model. The core challenge is the dual exploration of physical environment semantics and social landscape, where discoveries in one directly influence strategy in the other.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Coexistence Theory for Robust Multi-Agent Equilibrium
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from theoretical ecology, specifically the study of species coexistence, to the problem of opponent modeling and equilibrium in stochastic games. In ecology, stable coexistence is maintained not by a single fixed state but through mechanisms like temporal niche partitioning and environmental stochasticity, which prevent competitive exclusion. We formalize multi-agent interactions by viewing the strategic landscape as a fluctuating environment where 'resources' are opportunities for payoff. The adapted solution is to compute robust meta-strategies that ensure strategic diversity—a set of agent policies that can coexist over the long run. This is achieved by identifying and enforcing necessary differences in strategic 'niches' (e.g., exploration rates, planning depths, or model granularity) within the stochastic game framework, leading to equilibria that are robust to environmental noise and preserve a population of viable strategies rather than converging to a single, potentially brittle, policy.

ideas.title: Analyze and Benchmark Agent Architectures for Long-Horizon Planning in Destructible Environments
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the empirical analysis of competitive agent strategies from NIPS 2018 with the theoretical challenge of planning under destructible terrain. The core activity is to design and host a new benchmark competition or simulation suite where agents must perform long-horizon tasks in environments like bomb-defusal or mining, where terrain destruction is central. The key innovation is to then rigorously analyze the submitted agents to determine which architectural components (e.g., hierarchical planners, model-based RL, memory mechanisms) and training paradigms best handle the compounded uncertainty of a dynamically shrinking/altering map. This moves beyond post-hoc analysis of a static game and beyond developing a single algorithm, instead creating a comparative science of agent design for open-ended, state-altering planning.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Fidelity Planning under Dual Uncertainty in Actions and Terrain Dynamics
ideas.description: This research develops a hierarchical planning framework that accounts for two layers of uncertainty: the agent's actions and the stochastic, partially observable effects of those actions on destructible terrain. The core algorithm uses a coarse, long-horizon planner with probabilistic destruction models to generate strategic goals, while a fine-grained, short-horizon reactive planner continuously integrates real-time sensor data to correct the destruction model and execute robust, contingent maneuvers. This enables the agent to reason about the value of information, such as using a low-risk action to probe terrain stability, and to maintain plan viability even when destruction patterns are non-deterministic or initially unknown.

ideas.title: Goal-Driven Negotiation for Dynamic Multi-Agent Alliances
ideas.description: This research designs a multi-agent environment where the global task is decomposed into interdependent sub-tasks, each requiring a specific coalition to complete. Agents have private, potentially conflicting final objectives but must dynamically form negotiated alliances to access necessary sub-task resources. The system enforces explicit offer-acceptance protocols and shared reward distributions for sub-tasks, making alliance formation and dissolution a core, rational component of the policy. This creates a structured testbed for studying trust, commitment, and betrayal in multi-agent learning, moving beyond arbitrary team switching to goal-oriented coalition dynamics.

ideas.title: Continuous Control in Game Agents via Policy Gradients and Action Parameterization
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the common assumption of discrete action spaces in game AI (e.g., for movement, bomb placement, and timing). Drawing an analogy from robotics and continuous control—where agents like robotic arms or drones operate in inherently continuous action spaces—we adapt their solutions. In robotics, policy gradient algorithms (e.g., PPO, DDPG) are used to learn policies that output parameters for continuous distributions or direct control signals. Adapting this, we propose developing game agents whose policy networks output continuous values: a 2D velocity vector for movement, a continuous (x,y) coordinate within the game environment for bomb placement, and a real-valued delay for action timing. This shift from a discrete set of actions to a parameterized continuous space enables more fluid, precise, and strategically complex agent behaviors, such as subtle maneuvering, exact explosive placement, and variable timing tactics, fundamentally expanding the agent's strategic repertoire.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Game Theory Analysis of Two-Agent Pure Competition
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from evolutionary biology's study of two-species competition to the original seed idea of a simplified one-versus-one duel. In biology, complex ecosystems are scaled down to pairwise interactions (like predator-prey or direct competitors) using models such as the Lotka-Volterra equations and evolutionary game theory to find stable states and Evolutionary Stable Strategies (ESS). The adapted idea applies this analogous solution to a fully competitive, two-agent scenario in a strategic context. It involves formally defining the pure duel as a game with explicit strategy spaces and payoff structures, then employing evolutionary game theory to solve for Nash Equilibria and ESS. The research will focus on analyzing the dynamics of strategy adoption, mapping the basins of attraction for different stable outcomes, and fundamentally characterizing the conditions that lead to dominance, coexistence, or cyclic dynamics in this most elementary competitive unit.

ideas.title: Real-Time Multi-Agent Exploration and Coordination in Unbounded Procedural Worlds
ideas.description: This research direction investigates multi-agent reinforcement learning in a simulated, procedurally generated environment of effectively infinite scale, where agents operate in real-time and asynchronously. Agents must explore, gather information, and achieve complex goals without a global clock or state synchronization, forcing them to develop robust communication protocols and handle partial, outdated observations. The core challenge is scaling cooperative decision-making to a spatiotemporal continuum, where planning horizons are open-ended and the world state continuously evolves outside any agent's local perception. This tests generalization not just to new map layouts, but to fundamentally novel temporal and coordination dynamics unseen in smaller, turn-based settings.

ideas.title: Systematic Adversarial Curriculum for Robust Spatial Generalization
ideas.description: This research tests agent generalization by training on a dynamic sequence of procedurally generated maps where the generation parameters are controlled along distinct axes to build a curriculum of increasing complexity. An adversarial generator actively creates maps that break the agent's current policy, forcing the development of more robust skills. Final evaluation rigorously measures generalization on a diverse, held-out test set including maps designed by humans and maps from a novel, non-adversarial generation family, ensuring the agent learns fundamental spatial reasoning rather than overfitting to the training generator's quirks.

ideas.title: Dynamic Alliance Formation via Ecological Niche Construction
ideas.description: This idea inverts the fixed cooperative-competitive team structure by drawing an analogy from ecology and evolutionary biology. In ecosystems, species engage in transient, context-dependent mutualisms based on immediate needs and environmental pressures, a process explained by niche construction theory. The analogous solution is the dynamic, environment-mediated formation of cooperative networks. Adapting this, the research proposes designing multi-agent systems where agents can modify a shared 'interaction environment,' such as a communication protocol or resource landscape. Alliances then form and dissolve dynamically in response to these agent-constructed environmental states, making team structures fluid and emergent for the duration of an episode, challenging the assumption that cooperation requires stable, pre-defined teams.

ideas.title: Adaptive Planning for Destructible Environments Using Ecological Succession Models
ideas.description: This research adapts models from ecology, specifically those used for landscape dynamics and succession following disturbances like fires, to the problem of planning under uncertainty with destructible terrain. The analogous ecological solution uses probabilistic state-transition models (e.g., Markov models) to forecast how habitats change and recover over time. Adapted to this context, the terrain is modeled as a grid of cells, each with a probabilistic state machine representing its structural integrity (e.g., solid, cracked, rubble, temporarily impassable). Bomb explosions act as disturbances, triggering stochastic transitions in nearby cells. The core research challenge is to develop long-horizon planning algorithms that account for this dynamic, uncertain map evolution. The planner must evaluate action sequences not only by their immediate effects but by how they reshape the future probability landscape of navigable space, optimizing for goals that may only be achievable after the terrain has changed in uncertain ways.

ideas.title: Adversarial Game-Theoretic Planning with Strategic Terrain Destruction
ideas.description: This research develops algorithms for two-player, zero-sum duels in environments with fully destructible terrain. It focuses on long-horizon planning where each agent must reason not only about their own and their opponent's movement under uncertainty, but also about the optimal placement of bombs to alter the map for strategic advantage—cutting off routes, creating traps, or exposing the opponent. The core challenge is solving a combined game of movement and targeted terrain modification, requiring advances in adversarial search and game theory that account for a dynamically evolving state space shaped by the players' own destructive actions.

ideas.title: Adapting Cryptographic Commitment Schemes for Agent Communication Under Deception
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from cryptography—specifically commitment schemes—to the problem of agent communication under partial observability and potential deception. In cryptography, commitment protocols (e.g., Pedersen commitments) allow a party to bind themselves to a value while keeping it secret until a later reveal phase, ensuring both hiding and binding properties. This analogous solution is adapted by designing and testing multi-agent communication protocols where agents can cryptographically commit to future actions, intentions, or shared world-model updates without immediate full disclosure. The research would implement and evaluate these protocols in simulated partially observable environments, measuring how verifiable but delayed revelation affects cooperation efficiency, trust dynamics, and resilience against deceptive agents, thereby creating a new class of communication primitives for AI agents.

ideas.title: Competitive Cooperative Duel: A Two-Player Bomb Defusal Game with Asymmetric Information and Forced Coordination
ideas.description: This research designs a two-player game where each player is presented with a unique, private segment of a single bomb's puzzle. The players are in a duel, with victory conditions that are inherently competitive (e.g., being the sole survivor, gaining more points), but the bomb will detonate and cause mutual loss if they fail to coordinate and share their critical, asymmetric information to solve the combined puzzle. This creates a dynamic tension where players must strategically decide when to truthfully cooperate to avoid mutual destruction and when to withhold or deceive to gain a competitive edge within the shared, escalating time pressure. The core study focuses on the emergence of complex negotiation, trust dynamics, and meta-reasoning in this simplified yet deeply strategic hybrid scenario.

ideas.title: Mixed-Motive Multi-Agent Bomb Disposal with Emergent Negotiation
ideas.description: This research designs bomb-defusal environments where multiple AI agents have partially aligned and partially conflicting objectives, such as a shared goal to prevent explosion but competing goals over credit, resource allocation, or secondary private tasks. The puzzles require communication and coordinated action under time pressure, but agents must also negotiate to resolve incentive conflicts, building and breaking trust dynamically. The system measures not just puzzle-solving success, but the robustness of emergent coordination protocols, the efficiency of resource bargaining, and stability against betrayal or communication failure. This creates a richer testbed for studying the foundations of cooperative AI in realistic, non-zero-sum settings.

ideas.title: Distributed Cryptographic Key Management as a Cooperative Bomb Puzzle
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from threshold cryptography to create a cooperative-only puzzle variant. The core mechanic is that the final code to disarm a bomb-based puzzle is a secret distributed among multiple agents using a scheme like Shamir's Secret Sharing. Each agent must first solve a unique, localized sub-puzzle to obtain their individual 'share' of the secret. Crucially, no single share is useful alone. Agents must then work within a scaled, dual-constraint system (e.g., limited communication rounds, presence of deceptive 'traitor' agents, or verification puzzles) to securely transmit and combine a sufficient quorum of shares to reconstruct the final disarm code. This enforces mandatory cooperation, as failure or non-participation by any required agent makes successful disarmament mathematically impossible, directly mirroring the security guarantees of distributed cryptographic systems.

ideas.title: Continuous Cooperative Bomb Manipulation Puzzles
ideas.description: This research direction designs cooperative-only puzzle environments where agents must solve challenges using bombs with fully continuous action spaces for movement, placement, force, and timing. Agents must precisely coordinate their continuous actions—such as simultaneously applying forces to roll a bomb, timing staggered explosions to chain reactions, or sculpting terrain with blasts—to achieve shared objectives. This necessitates the development of policies that can perform fine-grained motor coordination while dynamically interpreting and adapting to a partner's real-time continuous actions, moving beyond discrete signaling. The core challenge is learning emergent, fluid teamwork in a high-dimensional action space where solutions require tight, synchronous control rather than pre-defined turn-taking or discrete command sequences.
